title: Introduction to Accounting
description: An introduction to accounting applied to business organizations. Emphasis is on the uses of accounting information in decision-making by internal and external users.

title: Principles of Accounting
description: Accounting 202 is a continuation of Accounting 201. Additional principles and practices of accountancy are introduced. Emphasis will continue to be placed on the uses of accounting information in decision-making by internal and external users.

title: Managerial Accounting
description: Analysis and interpretation of accounting data approached from the managerial viewpoint, with emphasis on planning and control, and decision-making.

title: Topics in Accounting
description: Covers a variety of topics to be determined by faculty interest and student demand.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: Development and implementation of the conceptual and practical aspects of valuation and income determination, time value of money, accounting changes, inventory valuation, and current and noncurrent assets. Special emphasis on GAAP as it relates to topics being covered.

title: Intermediate Accounting II
description: Development and implementation of the conceptual and practical aspects of valuation and income determination, with emphasis on intangible assets, retained earnings and contributed capital, diluted earnings per share, pensions, leases, deferred taxes, current and long-term liabilities, statement of cash flows, financial statement analysis, interim and segment reporting. Special emphasis on GAAP as it relates to topics being covered.

title: Auditing
description: Basic coverage of all steps in creating audit programs according to the audit risk model, and current issues in auditing. Specific coverage includes company risk evaluation, internal control, company analysis, and audit reporting.

title: Cost Accounting
description: Principles of cost accounting, emphasizing analysis of materials, labor, and factory overhead; application of standard costing to job order and process cost systems; analysis of cost behavior in the manufacturing firm with exposure to cost-profit-volume relationships, break-even analysis, and direct costing.

title: Individual Federal Income Taxation
description: Federal taxation on individuals including gross income, exclusions, deductions, exemptions, capital gains and property transactions. Tax return problems and tax research are included in this course.

title: Topics in Accounting
description: Covers a variety of topics to be determined by faculty interest and student demand.

title: Advanced Financial Accounting
description: Accounting for multiunit business enterprises; consolidated balance sheets, income statements, and statement of cash flows; Security and Exchange Commission reporting; foreign currency transactions and translation; international operations; and liquidation of partnerships.

title: Financial Accounting Integration
description: This course covers a variety of complex topics in financial accounting and reporting and is designed for students who expect entering to accounting practices and/or preparing for a CPA exam.

title: Governmental and Nonprofit Organization Accounting
description: An introduction to accounting principles for governmental, quasi-governmental, and nonprofit organizations. Includes budgetary and financial reporting requirements as well as the compliance requirements related to these entities. The course also introduces government and nonprofit audit requirements, and preparation of the nonprofit tax return.

title: Fraud Investigation and Prevention
description: Coverage of fraud methods, fraud investigation, and fraud prevention through internal controls. Emphasis on financial and asset misappropriation frauds.

title: Corporate, Partnership, and Other Entity Tax
description: Federal income taxation of entities including C corporations, partnership, and other business forms of taxation. Emphasis is on the Internal Revenue Code as it relates to business entities.

title: Ethics in Accounting
description: An introduction to ethics and professional responsibility in accounting. Coverage includes models of ethical reasoning, ethical dilemmas in accounting, and creating solutions to those ethical dilemmas.

title: Advanced Cost Accounting
description: A continuation of Accounting 314, with special emphasis on managerial profit analysis, planning, and control; direct costs, distributed costs by-product and joint product costs; differential and comparative costs, and variances.

title: Accounting Information Systems and Technology
description: Analysis and design of accounting information systems, including internal controls, data storage, management and analysis, spreadsheet applications, and reporting.

title: Data Analytics for Accounting
description: This course addresses the impact of Data Analytics on accounting. This course is lab oriented and will cover lab practices to solve business and accounting problems using Excel, Tableau, and Microsoft SQL.

title: Accounting Internship
description: Internship experience in business situations relevant to the accounting major under supervision of a faculty member.

title: Topics in Accounting
description: Covers a variety of topics to be determined by faculty interest and student demand.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Foundations in American Indian and Indigenous Studies
description: A diversity of disciplinary perspectives grounding the experiences of American Indian nations, peoples, and communities through American Indian perspectives, including principles rooted in concepts of sovereignty and indigenousness. Global connections expand on the shared commonalities with other indigenous peoples of the world.

title: Introduction to American Indian Expressive Cultures
description: Introduction to a broad range of American Indian expressive cultures, from traditional to contemporary forms, in art, literature, oral traditions, music, and film.

title: Studies in American Indian Languages I
description: Introduction to American Indian language families and concentrated study of one specific language within its cultural context, with emphasis on speaking and listening skills. (For specific languages, see current Class Schedule.)

title: Studies in American Indian Languages II
description: Continuation of American Indian Languages I, with additional attention paid to dialect diversity, and competing writing systems. (For specific languages, see current Class Schedule.)

title: Beginning Ojibwe I
description: A foundation in spoken and written Ojibwe language, listening and reading comprehension, grammatical usage, and the cultural backgrounds and experiences of Ojibwe people.

title: Beginning Ojibwe II
description: Continuation of Beginning Ojibwe I. Further developing elementary speaking, writing, and reading Ojibwe language skills.

title: Introduction to American Indian Literatures
description: A study of the literatures by and about American Indians, with special attention to the cultural background.

title: Ojibwe Culture and History
description: Introduction to the culture of the Ojibwe people. Students will learn about Ojibwe history, philosophy, culture, and contemporary communities.

title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
description: Culture as the human means of environmental adaptation. Explores cross-cultural regularities and the range of variation in human behavior. Comparison of widely different economic, social, political, and religious systems.

title: Intermediate Ojibwe I
description: Continuation of Beginning Ojibwe II. Build on speaking, listening, and reading skills. Learn techniques for using Ojibwe in everyday life.

title: Intermediate Ojibwe II
description: Continuation of Intermediate Ojibwe I. Focuses on developing skills necessary to aid language revitalization efforts.

title: American Indian History
description: An introduction to the history of the native peoples of the United States with major emphasis upon European contact, the mutual interaction of European and American Indian cultures, and the resulting adaptations and conflicts.

title: The American Indian in Literature and Film
description: A critical, cultural examination of the origins and evolution of film images of American Indians; analysis based on study and application of film techniques as well as literary and historical textual analysis.

title: Introduction to Contemporary American Indian Communities
description: Prepares students for intercultural immersion experiences through an introduction to American Indian cultural practices and key federal policies that have shaped contemporary societies.

title: American Indian Literature of Healing
description: Introduction to Indigenous philosophies around illness and narratives of healing as we read and discuss a variety of American Indian literary forms: fiction, non-fiction, poetry, and traditional Indigenous narratives. We will pay special attention to historical and cultural contexts that both predicate illness and indicate pathways to healing and balance within the self and the community.

title: American Indian Politics
description: The course will examine the recent trends in American Indian politics beginning with the American Indian Movement through the present. The course will include topics on tribal sovereignty, legalized gambling, and tribal government.

title: Tribal Nation Governments
description: Introduces students to Tribal governments as they exist today, including governmental structure, common business practices, citizenship, jurisdiction and interaction with other governments. Addresses the impacts of government policies and legal decisions on Tribal governments.

title: Chippewa Reserved Treaty Rights
description: This course is an in-depth study of the Chippewa treaties and addresses usufructuary rights and how treaties extend to issues such as pipelines and mining.

title: Special Topics
description: Topics will be determined by specific themes related to American Indian Studies by instructor and program director, and based upon student interest and need.

title: Native Geographies
description: The historical and contemporary relationships of American Indians to place, including land use and environmental issues, Indigenous mapping techniques, sacred sites, territorial nationhood, and legal rights to ceded and sovereign lands. The course also provides insight into the unique cultures of Indigenous peoples around the world.

title: Survey of North American Indian Art
description: A survey of North American Indian artwork in contemporary and historical contexts.

title: Indigenous Religions of the Americas
description: This course will introduce indigenous religions in the cultural contexts of Latin America and North American Indian nations. It will study historical religious development from Pre-Columbian, through colonial experience, to present day cultural movements.

title: Ancient Art and Architecture of Mexico and Central America
description: A study of the art and architecture of ancient Mexico and Central America between 2000 BCE - 1600 CE. Focus is on the materials, techniques, styles, and iconography of the art and architecture in relation to the contexts of production, use, and reception.

title: Art of Tawantinsuyo
description: A study of art created during the Inka Empire and the early Spanish Empire in western South America.

title: Contemporary American Indian Communities
description: Preparation for future study in AIS and/or work in American Indian communities by providing skills to work with communities toward mutually beneficial goals. Learn challenges facing American Indian communities and successful initiatives combatting those challenges. Travel required.

title: Traditional Ojibwe Stories
description: Students study Ojibwe stories, including the various ways those stories have been appropriated by outsiders, and learn ways to support Indigenous cultural revitalization.

title: American Indian Autobiography
description: Explores evolving forms and political implications of the creation and production of American Indian autobiographies and collaborative personal narratives.

title: Major Works in American Indian Literature
description: Major and supporting works of American Indian literature, from oral and written sources.

title: Studies in American Indian History
description: Selected topics depending on student and staff interests. Possible topics include the history of Wisconsin Indians and American Indians in the twentieth century.

title: Wisconsin American Indian History
description: Study of American Indian communities in Wisconsin with emphasis on the mix of continuity and change in response to outside forces.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of problem or topic related to American Indian Studies.

title: Independent Study
description: Original individual research or studies related to American Indian Studies.

title: Indigenous Theory and Methodologies
description: Introduction to Indigenous theory and methods of critical practice within the discipline. Emphasis on active research and critical analysis of current texts, discussions, and issues within Indigenous scholarship.

title: Capstone
description: The capstone course asks a student to integrate previous learning in American Indian Studies to produce a major research project or creative activity within a seminar format. Working with an American Indian community/group/individual is recommended, which may include travel.

title: Special Topics
description: Topics will be determined by specific themes related to American Indian Studies by instructor and program director, and based upon student interest and need. Possible topics include treaty rights conflicts, tribal sovereignty, Indian mascots and logos, and studies in American Indian authors.

title: Internship
description: Supervised assignment outside of the University to health or education institutions or agencies, to American Indian communities, to research institutions, to museums, cultural institutions, or other determined by supervising instructor.

title: Independent Study
description: Original individual research or studies related to American Indian Studies.

title: Native Geographies
description: The historical and contemporary relationships of American Indians to place, including land use and environmental issues, Indigenous mapping techniques, sacred sites, territorial nationhood, and legal rights to ceded and sovereign lands. The course also provides insight into the unique cultures of Indigenous peoples around the world.

title: Contemporary American Indian Communities
description: Preparation for future study in AIS and/or work in American Indian communities by providing skills to work with communities toward mutually beneficial goals. Learn challenges facing American Indian communities and successful initiatives combatting those challenges. Travel required.

title: Studies in American Indian History
description: Selected topics depending on student and staff interests. Possible topics include the history of Wisconsin Indians and American Indians in the twentieth century.

title: Wisconsin American Indian History
description: Study of American Indian communities in Wisconsin with emphasis on the mix of continuity and change in response to outside forces.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of problem or topic related to American Indian Studies.

title: Indigenous Theory and Methodologies
description: Introduction to Indigenous theory and methods of critical practice within the discipline. Emphasis on active research and critical analysis of current texts, discussions, and issues within Indigenous scholarship.

title: Independent Study
description: Original individual research or studies related to American Indian Studies.

title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
description: Culture as the human means of environmental adaptation. Explores cross-cultural regularities and the range of variation in human behavior. Comparison of widely different economic, social, political, and religious systems.

title: Introduction to Physical Anthropology
description: Investigates human biocultural evolution, through comparative analysis of fossils, contemporary primates, and genetics.

title: Introduction to Archaeology
description: Human cultural prehistory as inferred from archaeological evidence, from the earliest Pleistocene cultures to the development of urban life. Explores archaeological methods and techniques.

title: Special Topics
description: Topics will vary depending on interests of students and faculty.

title: Space, Society, Subject
description: Broad introduction to social theory in anthropology and geography with an emphasis on contemporary theoretical approaches to social, spatial, and cultural dynamics and their intersections.

title: Culture and Transnational Dynamics
description: Critical discussion of conceptual approaches to cultural processes, differences, and encounters from a transnational perspective.

title: Language in Culture and Society
description: Examination of the rules and patterns for speech behavior in a variety of socio-cultural formations. Language as expressive and formative of social status, social identity, and group membership. Study of multilingualism and language planning.

title: North American Indians
description: Comparative analysis, by culture area, of traditional American Indian economies, social and political organization, religion and philosophy. Impact of European colonization and control on traditional cultures.

title: Directed Studies
description: Directed study of an anthropological problem approved by the supervising instructor and department chair.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: An intensive study of an anthropological topic resulting in a quality paper or report.

title: Anthropological Fieldwork Methods and Research Design
description: This seminar offers a broad introduction to the subject of fieldwork in sociocultural anthropology, with the goal of training students to undertake independent research. Foci include methodology, field experience, and outstanding research examples.

title: Religion and Culture
description: Examination of the nature of human religious expression in cross-cultural contexts. Focus on the ways that people use myths, symbols, and rituals in both oral and written traditions.

title: Special Topics
description: Topics will vary depending on interests of students and faculty. Proposed topics include American Indian religion and philosophy.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: An intensive study of an anthropological topic resulting in a quality paper or report.

title: Space, Society, Subject
description: Broad introduction to social theory in anthropology and geography with an emphasis on contemporary theoretical approaches to social, spatial, and cultural dynamics and their intersections.

title: Culture and Transnational Dynamics
description: Critical discussion of conceptual approaches to cultural processes, differences, and encounters from a transnational perspective.

title: Language in Culture and Society
description: Examination of the rules and patterns for speech behavior in a variety of socio-cultural formations. Language as expressive and formative of social status, social identity, and group membership. Study of multilingualism and language planning.

title: Anthropological Fieldwork Methods and Research Design
description: This seminar offers a broad introduction to the subject of fieldwork in sociocultural anthropology, with the goal of training students to undertake independent research. Foci include methodology, field experience, and outstanding research examples.

title: Introduction to the Visual Arts
description: Visual arts survey to develop awareness and appreciation of theories, methods, and components of art within cultural and historical frameworks.

title: Foundations: Drawing and Composition
description: Introduction to freehand observational drawing and visual composition using a variety of materials and techniques.

title: Foundations: Two-Dimensional Design
description: Fundamental development of the effective use of design elements and principles in visual compositions applied through a variety of materials and techniques.

title: Foundations: Three-Dimensional Art
description: Introduction to three-dimensional methods and materials. Includes historical and contemporary perspectives of 3-D art.

title: Foundations: Digital Frameworks
description: Provides a digital foundation for application across the visual arts upon which students build a creative and technical toolkit. Design principles (e.g., layout and composition) are emphasized and students develop adaptive strategies and skills in the visualization of dimension, motion, and time. The course provides an introduction to Adobe and other digital design software.

title: Intermediate Drawing
description: Application of observational and expressive intermediate drawing techniques. Includes an introduction to live figure drawing.

title: Introduction to Painting
description: Exploration of subjects and concepts that introduce the technical and expressive nature of the painting medium.

title: Introduction to Printmaking
description: Production of printed media with basic exploration in various printmaking areas. Includes relief, intaglio, serigraphy, and lithography.

title: Introduction to Ceramics
description: Introductory experience with basic ceramic techniques and approaches focusing on hand-building, glazing, and firing.

title: Beginning Sculpture
description: Hands-on exploration of a variety of materials, techniques, and concepts related to contemporary sculpture.

title: Typography
description: This course introduces typography as an essential framework of visual communication and graphic design. Students will study the history and evolution of typography and investigate its key components, including fundamentals of letterforms, type specifications and terminology, composition and formatting, and entanglements of type and image.

title: 3-D Computer Modeling
description: An introductory course exploring 3-D computer image making techniques in modeling, lighting, and rendering as well as learning digital 3-D animation concepts and processes.

title: Introduction to Photography as an Art Form
description: A studio art course in creating photographs. Includes: concepts, analysis of works and development of composition and technique. Camera work, image processing, professional printing. Digitally based, color and black and white.

title: BFA Review Preparation
description: Course informs and prepares pre-Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) students for their BFA review. Students review, select, and document work, develop an artist statement, and work through possibilities for creative direction. Students think creatively and independently about how to direct their academic plans and career goals into creative work leading up to their final BFA senior exhibition. This course is intended to be taken the semester of the BFA review, which takes place independently of the course.

title: Special Topics
description: Interest topics not covered in regular art courses such as specific media subjects, creation procedures, contemporary art concerns, or other issues.

title: Art Therapy
description: This course introduces the history, theories, principles and practices of art therapy. Techniques and ethical practices in art therapy and the helping professions will be explored in relation to various theories in healing and psychology. Students will explore art media as it relates to therapeutic themes and experiences as they simultaneously explore and develop their own artistic identity.

title: Design Across the Disciplines
description: Empowers students of all disciplines to visually communicate information and ideas to serve and support their professional fields. Students learn about design theory, design psychology, the creative process, and design thinking methodologies. Course projects use digital design tools such as Adobe software as well as hands on design techniques.

title: Illustration Narratives
description: Exploration of the visual narrative using images and text within the context of contemporary illustration.

title: Drawing: Conceptual Practices
description: Contemporary approaches to drawing at the intermediate level with a focus on interdisciplinary concepts including collaboration, electronic media, and animation.

title: Figure Drawing
description: Develop drawing techniques with various media and enhance personal expression in depicting the human body while observing a live model.

title: Illustration Editorials
description: Investigation of current events, news editorial markets, and promotional markets in relation to illustration.

title: Painting: Space and Process
description: Exploration of painting techniques and processes in relation to pictorial and physical space using various materials.

title: Water-Media Process
description: Painting approaches utilizing water-media. Emphasis on material processes and experimentation.

title: Water-Media Themes
description: Application of thematic frameworks using water-media.

title: Painting: Conceptual and Aesthetic Strategies
description: Utilize problem-solving and research strategies with painting techniques to develop individual direction. Emphasis on conceptual development.

title: Intermediate Ceramics
description: Application of wheel-throwing techniques as a means of visual expression. Emphasis on glazes, clay, and loading and firing kilns.

title: Intermediate Sculpture
description: Continuation of contemporary sculptural practices with emphasis on combined methods and techniques of mixed media.

title: Site-Specific Art
description: Students will explore how site-specific art incorporates space, place, and environmental/cultural features as they create artwork that interacts with a specific location. Site-specific art is integrative, draws from a wide range of media, and can be situated in interior or exterior spaces, on natural or built landscapes, and be produced from miniature to monumental scales.

title: Found Media
description: Course will focus on the integration, manipulation, and conceptualization of "found media" into sculpture.

title: Motion Graphics
description: Exploration of two-dimensional digital motion graphics. Emphasis on identity applications, internet motion graphics, and animation.

title: Graphic Design: Concepts and Experimentation
description: Application of advanced design principles to develop promotional materials. Emphasis on diversity, equity, and inclusivity in graphic communication.

title: User Experience and Web Design
description: Introduces students to topics like human-computer factors, user research practices, and design thinking methodologies required for user experience designers. Students apply these knowledge frameworks as they research, design, test, and develop a mobile digital product for a conceptual, tech-savvy business client.

title: Identity Development and Design
description: Students design a comprehensive visual expression of a brand and create a system that supports it. Students practice researching, analyzing, developing, designing, proposing, and building out a comprehensive library of elements that represent a professional brand identity system.

title: Digital Painting
description: Create representational and non-representational artworks with digital software and technologies. Emphasis on conceptual development and discussion.

title: 3-D Computer Animation I
description: Continued development of computer 3-D modeling skills begun in Art 278. Students study comprehensive creative concepts and advanced 3-D artistic modeling for objects and life-figures. Additional animation techniques are included.

title: Intermediate Photography: Darkroom
description: Continued work with photography genres, concepts, and processes. Students learn black-and-white film developing and printing and alternative processes such as silver emulsion (Liquid Light), Cyanotype, and pinhole to expand their creativity and develop individual direction. Emphasis on conceptual development.

title: Making Pictures: Contemporary Directions in Photographic Art
description: Students continue to develop their photographic skills and artistic visions as they learn new and traditional techniques and discover innovative applications of photography. Students build on digital SLR-camera skills as they explore alternative image-making methods such as film, smart phone, and other lens-based media and integrate photography with other art processes, media, and approaches.

title: Video for Art and Design
description: Explore theoretical, critical, aesthetic, and historic issues as they relate to time based imagery in contemporary art practice. Students develop technical and visual tools necessary to execute and articulate conceptual ideas and personal visions.

title: Artist Books: Image & Text
description: Interdisciplinary exploration of art in the book form. Examines the conventions of the written word, the image, and image-text relations. Explores book structures and designs through bookbinding demonstration and hands-on practices.

title: Directed Studies
description: Approved study of a specific studio art or design topic/problem.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual art project under the direction of an art faculty member involving research and application culminating in a final product or report.

title: Advanced Studio Practice
description: Studio course mixing advanced studio students of various disciplines who are researching individually proposed projects. Emphasizes the professional presentation of artwork.

title: Advanced Ceramics
description: Personal exploration of advanced ceramic forms. Emphasizes professional presentation of artwork. Topics rotate when offered.

title: Digital Design: Expanded Media
description: Application and integration of visual communication in electronic media. Key areas include digital animation, motion graphics, sound design, foley and sound effects, and interactive art.

title: Design for Community and People
description: Students develop and design professional print and digital collateral for community business partners. Students work directly with community clients and create real-world design solutions, including flexible and modular design solutions/systems using a variety of digital design tools.

title: Portfolio and Professional Development
description: Students prepare for post-graduation by contextualizing and publishing their work, curating content, and communicating critical professional information about themselves in a published online professional portfolio.

title: BFA Senior Exhibition
description: Capstone course for all senior Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) students in the Department of Art & Design. Students prepare their thesis project and work with instructors and peers to realize a professional exhibition. Successful completion of this course satisfies BFA Senior Exhibition requirement.

title: Special Topics
description: Interest topics not covered in regular art courses such as specific media subjects, creation procedures, contemporary art concerns, or other issues.

title: Student Academic Apprenticeship
description: Facilitate teaching in a specific course as supervised by art faculty. Enhance personal knowledge of art and ability to communicate it effectively.

title: Internship
description: Applied knowledge and practical experience in professional fields. Work employs technical, analytical, and educational aspects of art and design.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual art project under the direction of an art faculty member involving research and application culminating in a final product or report.

title: Directed Studies
description: Approved study of a specific studio art or design topic/problem.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual art project under the direction of an art faculty member involving research and application culminating in a final product or report.

title: Art of the 20th Century
description: Study of art and architecture during the 20th century. Emphasizes global and pluralistic nature of modern and post-modern art.

title: Global Art History to 1400
description: An introduction to the fundamental concepts of art history through a representative group of artworks created in ancient and medieval contexts around the world.

title: Global Art History Since 1400
description: An introduction to the fundamental concepts of art history through a representative group of artworks created between 1400 and the present day around the world.

title: Special Topics
description: Interest topics not covered in regular art history courses such as historical themes, period studies, national/regional traditions, or other focuses.

title: Ancient Greek, Etruscan, and Roman Art
description: A study of the art and architecture of the Greeks (900 BCE-31 BCE), Etruscans (700-100 BCE), and Romans (500 BCE-400 CE). The primary objective is the interpretation of artwork and architecture in their original contexts of production and reception.

title: Survey of North American Indian Art
description: A survey of North American Indian artwork in contemporary and historical contexts.

title: Themes in Contemporary Art
description: An investigation of the processes, theories, and historical viewpoints that motivate and structure the content of the current art world.

title: Ancient Art and Architecture of Mexico and Central America
description: A study of the art and architecture of ancient Mexico and Central America between 2000 BCE - 1600 CE. Focus is on the materials, techniques, styles, and iconography of the art and architecture in relation to the contexts of production, use, and reception.

title: Art of Tawantinsuyo
description: A study of art created during the Inka Empire and the early Spanish Empire in western South America.

title: History of Graphic Design
description: Designed as a survey of the development of European and American graphic design in the twentieth century. Focus is on the styles and philosophies of the most influential designers of this period.

title: Travel Opportunities in Art
description: Travel seminars examining art in this country and abroad. Ranges from field trips to full programs.

title: Photography: History and Theory
description: Survey examining ideas, processes, and theories within the history of still photography for the past and present. Defines relationships between the medium and other contemporary events that have shaped the nation and world.

title: Methodologies of Art History
description: Survey of major methodologies and approaches used by art historians to construct and interpret the discipline’s content.

title: Directed Studies
description: Approved study of a specific art historical problem or topic.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual research project in a specific art history subject under the direction of an art history faculty member.

title: Special Topics
description: Interest topics not covered in regular art history courses such as historical themes, period studies, national/regional traditions, or other focuses.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual research project in a specific art history subject under the direction of an art history faculty member.

title: Travel Opportunities in Art
description: Travel seminars examining art in this country and abroad. Ranges from field trips to full programs.

title: Foundations of Artificial Intelligence
description: Introduction to concepts related to the use and creation of AI. Students will gain an understanding of AI concepts and techniques, their ethical implications, explore transparency in AI models, and examine influence of AI on society and workforce.

title: AI in Drug Design
description: The use of artificial intelligence in chemistry and biology including applications such as drug design. Integration of high-performance computing and computational methods to search, visualize, and analyze chemical and biological information. Discussion of key ethical concerns in informatics-based models.

title: Human-Computer Interaction
description: Introduction to the design, implementation, and evaluation of interactive software and AI systems, with a focus on the intersection of ethics and computing. Topics include transparent and explainable AI, human abilities, user research, prototyping, evaluation techniques, design communication, and team skills.

title: AI in Healthcare
description: This course explores the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the healthcare industry, covering fundamental concepts, ethical considerations, and real-world applications. Students will gain an understanding of how AI is used in medical imaging, diagnostics, natural language processing, patient monitoring, and drug discovery.

title: Artificial Intelligence
description: A comprehensive exploration of Artificial Intelligence spanning classical to contemporary approaches. The course traces AI's evolution from symbolic reasoning and rule-based systems to current statistical and data-driven methodologies and examines crucial ethical considerations in AI, including data bias and societal impact.

title: Natural Language Processing and Generative AI
description: This course introduces students to the core principles and evolving landscape of Natural Language Processing (NLP). It begins with foundational techniques in text analysis and linguistic structures, providing a basis for understanding language data. Students will explore word representations and semantic models, progressing to cutting-edge architectures such as transformers and large language models.

title: AI-Driven Project Development
description: An exploration of crafting intelligent software solutions using state-of-the-art AI techniques. Practical projects focus on designing, developing, and deploying AI-powered applications while applying the principles of the software development life cycle.

title: Introduction to Robotics
description: Introduction to fundamentals of robotics engineering: programming, motion, sensors, and controls. Foundations of human-robot systems and the application of robotics to improve quality of life. Lab will include hands-on building and operation of robotic systems.

title: Sensors and Actuators
description: This course introduces the principles, design, and applications of sensors and actuators with a focus on their role in robotic and mechatronic systems. Students will study the physical foundations, operating characteristics, and performance parameters of a wide range of transducers and actuators, from classical devices to modern micro- and nano-scale technologies.

title: Control Theory and Applications
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles and practical applications of control theory, with an emphasis on assistive systems and robotic platforms. Students will explore classical control methods, such as PID and frequency-domain analysis, as well as modern control topics, including state-space modeling, observer design, and digital control.

title: System Integration in Assistive Robotics
description: This course explores the principles and practices of integrating sensors, actuators, and control systems into complete robotic platforms. Building on the foundations Sensors and Actuators and applying the concepts as taught in Control Theory and Applications, students will learn how to design, assemble, and validate multi-component robotic systems that operate autonomously and interact safely with humans and their environment.

title: ASRE Capstone I
description: First of a two-course capstone sequence. Hands-on, project-based experiences including engineering design, engineering standards and project management.

title: ASRE Capstone II
description: Second of a two-course capstone sequence. Conclusion of student-designed projects accompanied by preparation of multiple technical documents.

title: General Biology
description: An integrated presentation of important topics and ideas in modern biology. Designed to serve as a broad introduction to the life sciences.

title: Exploring the Living World
description: A lab and discussion course introducing students to various modes of inquiry in modern biology, ranging from the molecular to the ecological. Students will work in groups to develop, conduct and write up findings of original research projects.

title: Human Sexual Biology
description: Structure, function, control, and coordination of the human reproductive organ system. Formation and function of sex cells. Problems of lactation, fertility, sterility, sexuality, birth control challenges, and human population.

title: The Big Picture in Bioinformatics
description: Explores how a liberal education relates to bioinformatics, the impacts of the field on society, and ethical expectations of the discipline. Provides an overview of bioinformatics looking at the differences between being a user and a developer along with additional technical topics.

title: Biology of Humans
description: Basic principles and processes of the human body. Emphasis on human origin and evolution, biomolecules, cells, organ systems and disorders, reproduction, genetics, and the role of humans in the biosphere.

title: Environmental Biology and Conservation
description: An introduction to natural resource conservation. Problems associated with the use and abuse of America's resources are studied in an ecological framework. Resource management methods are explored.

title: Environmental Biology and Conservation Lab
description: Laboratory science course intended to be taken with Biology 180, Environmental Biology and Conservation, in order to receive laboratory science credit. Labs and field trips have students explore problems and solutions associated with environmental and conservation of natural resource issues.

title: Ecology of Sustainable Food Systems
description: An introduction to the ecological impacts and sustainability of various forms of agricultural systems. Emphasis is on contemporary and regional issues. Students will complete a service learning project with a not-for-profit community organization that applies course concepts.

title: Plants and Society
description: An introduction to the biology of plants and their use by humans for food, beverage, spices, commercial products, and medicine.

title: Human Nutrition
description: Introduction to nutrition including coverage of nutrients-functions and sources; digestion and absorption; food guides; obesity and energy needs; diet and chronic disease; hunger in the world and United States; organic foods; sport nutrition; nutrition.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I
description: The fundamentals of bodily function are studied at the cellular, tissue, organ, and organ system levels. Integration of physiological function and anatomical structure will be highlighted in the skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine systems.

title: Foundations of Biology I
description: Introduction to cell biology, genetics, evolution, and microbiology.

title: Foundations of Biology II
description: Introduction to evolution, organismal form and function, and ecology.

title: Foundations of Biological Inquiry
description: Introduction to inquiry methods in biology focusing on scientific methods including experimental design, data collection and analysis, and critical thinking.

title: Microbiology
description: Study of the attributes of several microbial groups and their interactions with human systems in the disease process.

title: Special Topics in Introductory Biology
description: Biological topics of current and special interest not typically covered in an introductory biology course, and of special interest to students and faculty.

title: Student Academic Experience
description: Experience for qualified students, selected and supervised by faculty members, to facilitate teaching and learning in a specific course. Students enhance their knowledge of biology and their ability to communicate that knowledge to other students.

title: Molecular and Cell Biology
description: Current concepts in molecular/cell biology including structure/function of cells and cell organelles, gene function/regulation, and cell signaling.

title: Infectious Disease Biology
description: Introduction to the realities of pathogens and parasites in our lives and their relationship to hosts, reservoirs, and environmental factors. Discussion of emerging and re-emerging diseases within the context of human, veterinary, wildlife, and planetary health.

title: Cell and Molecular Biology Laboratory
description: A lab course introducing various technical aspects of cell and molecular biology. Techniques include cell culture, cell separation, extraction and separation of macromolecules, immunoassay, enzyme assays, immunofluorescence, PCR, and DNA sequencing.

title: Evolution
description: An introduction to evolution, including: origin and impact of the theory of evolution, origin of life, genetics and natural selection, molecular evolution, speciation, adaptation, systematics, fossil record, biogeography, species interactions, and human evolution.

title: General Entomology
description: Study of the structure, function, diversity, ecology, and management of insects. Field collection and laboratory identification of terrestrial and aquatic insects of Wisconsin.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II
description: The fundamentals of bodily function are studied at the cellular, tissue, organ, and organ system levels. Integration of physiological function and anatomical structure will be highlighted in the cardiovascular, respiratory, excretory, digestive, and reproductive systems.

title: Reproductive Physiology
description: Examination of the principles and mechanisms of reproduction in human. Social aspects of reproduction, including reproductive biotechnologies and reproductive health will also be discussed.

title: Human Physiology
description: Analysis of how the human body's systems function and work together to maintain a stable internal environment.

title: Plant Form and Function
description: Study of the classification, structure, function, development, and behavior of plants in an evolutionary and experimental context.

title: Animal Form and Function
description: An examination of the comparative biology of animals including their evolution, reproduction, development, physiology, and behavior. Laboratories include an experimental approach to the study of animal form and behavior.

title: Studies in Tropical Environments
description: Extended travel to study the biological diversity and natural history of terrestrial and aquatic organisms from a tropical environment. Emphasis on human impacts to tropical ecosystems.

title: Ecology
description: Core concepts and theories in ecology at the individual, population, community, and ecosystems levels and their applications.

title: Genetics
description: Basic principles of heredity and variation; genetic systems, structure and roles of nucleic acids, mutation, allelism, genes in development, genes in populations, and genetics in human life; genetic engineering and genomics.

title: Genetics Inquiry
description: A lab/discussion course involving methods of genetic and genomic analysis.

title: Plant Systematics
description: Principles of classification, nomenclature, and determining evolutionary relationships among plants; survey of plant diversity including mosses, ferns, conifers, and flowering plants. Field identification of local flora.

title: Conservation Biology
description: Scientific basis for conserving biological diversity. Explore global efforts in research, education, and advocacy for conserving genes, species, and ecosystems and their ecological and evolutionary processes. Applied engagement in habitat restoration, conservation research, and education.

title: Biological Field Experiences
description: Group participation and collaboration with the community to engage in hands-on work experience or environmental education focusing on protecting/teaching about Wisconsin biodiversity through projects in habitat restoration, research, and education.

title: Field Botany
description: Field study of the flora and vegetation of Wisconsin. Includes sight recognition of plants, identification of unknown plants using keys, plant communities, an introduction to plant taxonomy, and specimen preparation.

title: Vegetation Ecology
description: Patterns and processes in vegetation, with an emphasis on the natural vegetation of Wisconsin. Laboratories will include field methods and an introduction to multivariate statistical analyses.

title: Applied Bioinformatics
description: Addresses tools and applications used in the analysis of biological sequence data. Topics include navigation of biological sequence databases, sequence-similarity searches, domain and motif prediction, sequence alignment, phylogenetic analyses, molecular evolution, and analysis of differential gene expression. Covers the biological and mathematical theory underlying these analyses, and provides practical, hands-on experience with computational tools implementing them.

title: Invertebrate Zoology
description: An introduction to invertebrate animals with emphases on phylogenetic relationships, form, function, and ecology. Laboratory sessions include field trips for the purpose of collecting invertebrate animals and experiments to learn about the biology of invertebrates.

title: Systems Neuroscience
description: How networks of neurons form the basis of higher brain function including sensory, motor, and integrative functions.

title: Systems Neuroscience Lab
description: Methods to study systems of neurons as they relate to behavior and cellular mechanisms including electrophysiology, histology and immunocytochemistry.

title: Vertebrate Biology
description: A course designed to provide a comparative overview of vertebrate diversity. Topics include the anatomy, physiology, behavior, and ecology of fishes, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals within an evolutionary context. For students seeking a general understanding of vertebrate biology and skills in field identification.

title: Cellular and Developmental Neuroscience
description: In-depth examination of molecular, cellular and developmental processes in the nervous system. Exposure to the primary literature. Students design, implement and interpret experiments in laboratory. Students will write a proposal based on work in laboratory.

title: Biology of Stress
description: Exploration of stress response, an important survival mechanism that is found in all vertebrates. Includes an overview of stress, recent literature related to the biological aspects of stress, the wider implications of stress in our society and the ways that we can cope with and minimize stress in our lives.

title: Biology of Microorganisms
description: Study of microbial taxonomy, physiology, genetics, ecology, and morphology with environmental, industrial, agricultural, and medical applications.

title: Field Zoology
description: Field trips and laboratories concerned with habitat observation, collection, and identification of local animals, with emphasis on the invertebrates and cold-blooded vertebrates.

title: Animal Behavior
description: Study of animal behavior with emphasis on evolutionary approaches; topics include feeding adaptations, predator-prey interactions, habitat selection, communication, sociality, and mating strategies. Students work together on lab/field exercises and conduct a semi-independent research project.

title: Medical Mycology
description: An introduction to the medically important fungi. Topics include fungal structure; superficial, cutaneous, and systemic mycoses; mechanisms of disease; host response to fungal infections and treatment/prevention.

title: Marine Biology
description: This course emphasizes evolutionary adaptations to the marine environment and interactions among marine organisms with their habitats. Communities studied include coral reefs, deep sea benthos, plankton, nekton, and intertidal assemblages. We rely heavily upon activities and discussion of the primary literature to make inferences about fundamental biological processes within the context of life in marine environments. Students will also be asked to consider the biological basis of major issues in marine conservation.

title: Aquatic Ecology
description: Ecology of aquatic organisms in lakes and streams. Emphasis is placed on processes shaping the structure and dynamics of freshwater communities. Laboratory experiments and field trips required.

title: Biology of Fishes
description: Anatomy, physiology, taxonomy, behavior, ecology, and evolution of fishes. Laboratory and field study of local freshwater species.

title: Endocrinology
description: Study of the relationships between histophysiology, chemistry, and disorders of the endocrine system; its interrelationships with the nervous system and how both contribute to bodily homeostasis.

title: Biostatistics
description: Practical applications of statistics are used to learn concepts and to develop skills. Graphical analysis, parameter estimation, and formal tests of hypotheses performed in the context of real biological studies using powerful statistical software.

title: Biological Investigation
description: Designed for students participating in undergraduate/faculty collaborative research projects.

title: Undergraduate Seminar
description: Presentation of current research topics in student-selected subareas of biology.

title: Biology Research Methods
description: Introduction to scientific inquiry. Focus on philosophy of science, nature of scientific data, methods of data collection, analysis of data and peer reviewed literature, and communication of scientific results.

title: Biological Immersion Experience
description: Project-based, field intensive, biological immersion experience focusing on the nature of environmental controls on floral and faunal distribution within a geographic region. Societal influence and interaction with the biological realm will be emphasized.

title: Directed Studies
description: Directed study of a question or problem of biological interest, approved by the supervising faculty and the department chair.

title: Honors Research
description: Individual research project under the direction of a research adviser.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Independent study projects under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Medical Microbiology
description: An introduction to microorganisms that cause disease. Topics will include the biology and ecology of medically important fungi, parasites, viruses and bacteria; mechanisms of disease; host responses to microbial infections; treatment and prevention of various disease.

title: Current Topics in Virology and Immunology
description: Lectures and readings in immunology and virology. Emphasis on the mode of thinking and reasoning that led to earlier solutions. Discussions of current advances in the fields.

title: Advanced Cell and Molecular Lab
description: A lab/discussion course teaching methods in molecular/cell biology research using prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems, focusing on DNA/RNA analyses.

title: Molecular Genetics
description: Molecular genetics comprehensively integrates biochemistry, molecular, and cellular biology and serves as the culminating biochemistry/molecular biology course. Topics include genomics, developmental genetic systems, organelle genomes, evolutionary genetics, immunogenetics, and medical genetics.

title: Collaborative Research in Biology
description: Students design and implement collaborative research projects to address special topics identified by the instructor. Student researchers will develop hypotheses and design studies to test them. The course emphasizes teamwork, data interpretation, and communication.

title: Issues in Biology
description: Senior groups will use their education to locate and evaluate research on selected issues. Groups will produce a scholarly background paper, a creative presentation, and lead a discussion.

title: Biology Honors Thesis
description: Research and writing/preparation of Honors Thesis. Students are expected to make substantial intellectual contributions to the development, implementation, or scholarly interpretation of the project.

title: Biological Field Studies
description: Extended travel to study field biology. Students record notes and data in the field and submit final written and/or oral reports. Field identification quizzes may also be required.

title: Special Topics
description: Lectures, laboratory, or field study covering specific areas not normally discussed in regular classes, and of special interest to students or faculty.

title: Student Academic Apprenticeship
description: For qualified students, selected and supervised by faculty members, to facilitate teaching and learning in a specific course. Apprentices enhance their knowledge of biology and their ability to communicate that knowledge to other students.

title: Senior Research Presentation
description: Capstone for biology majors involved in field or laboratory research. Student will present independent research through an oral presentation or poster given at Student Research Day.

title: Internship in Biology
description: Provides supervised on-the-job experience in a biologically-related area of interest.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Independent study projects under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Molecular and Cell Biology
description: Current concepts in molecular/cell biology including structure/function of cells and cell organelles, gene function/regulation, and cell signaling.

title: Infectious Disease Biology
description: Introduction to the realities of pathogens and parasites in our lives and their relationship to hosts, reservoirs, and environmental factors. Discussion of emerging and re-emerging diseases within the context of human, veterinary, wildlife, and planetary health.

title: General Entomology
description: Study of the structure, function, diversity, ecology, and management of insects. Field collection and laboratory identification of terrestrial and aquatic insects of Wisconsin.

title: Reproductive Physiology
description: Examination of the principles and mechanisms of reproduction in human. Social aspects of reproduction, including reproductive biotechnologies and reproductive health will also be discussed.

title: Biology of Microorganisms
description: Study of microbial taxonomy, physiology, genetics, ecology, and morphology with environmental, industrial, agricultural, and medical applications.

title: Endocrinology
description: Study of the relationships between histophysiology, chemistry, and disorders of the endocrine system; its interrelationships with the nervous system and how both contribute to bodily homeostasis.

title: Current Topics in Virology and Immunology
description: Lectures and readings in immunology and virology. Emphasis on the mode of thinking and reasoning that led to earlier solutions. Discussions of current advances in the fields.

title: Research Techniques
description: Emphasis on experimental design and specific techniques appropriate to the various subdisciplines in biology.

title: Directed Studies
description: Lectures, laboratory, or field study covering specific areas not normally included in regular classes, and of special interest to students and/or faculty.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects under direction of a faculty member.

title: Thesis
description: A description of acceptable topics and the precise nature of the thesis requirement is provided in the departmental program descriptions.

title: Introduction to Biomedical Engineering
description: Introduction to the field of biomedical engineering and the role of bioengineers. The class will cover a number of human diseases and discuss biomedical engineering techniques that are being developed to improve healthcare.

title: Research Experience - BME
description: This course provides research opportunities in Biomedical Engineering for students in their first or second year of their major studies.

title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Lecture and possibly laboratory or field work in the study of current topics in Biomedical Engineering of special interest to students and faculty.

title: Student Academic Apprenticeship
description: Student academic apprentices in biomedical engineering will assist the course instructor, will aid in the learning of students enrolled in the connected course, and will demonstrate learning about the instruction process and a deeper understanding of biomedical engineering.

title: 3D Printing in Medicine
description: This course will introduce students to the emerging use of 3D Printing in medicine. Students will learn the process of taking medical imaging data to the production of physical anatomic models. The course will cover the basics of imaging, methods used to create 3D computer models from imaging data, the processing of imaging files with CAD software and the conversion into files that are compatible with 3D printing.

title: Clinical Problems in Biomedical Engineering
description: Lectures and discussions to investigate advances in the use of engineering approaches to address key clinical problems that exist in the medical field. Engineering topics will include: bioinstrumentation, medical imaging, biomaterials, biomechanics and medical devices. This course builds on the Engineering and Biology principles introduced in BME 201 .

title: Biomedical Engineering Instruments and Measurements w/lab
description: Principles and the application of instrumentation that are used for medical measurements and observing physiological variables. The class will focus on the detection of signals, data acquisition and processing, and display of signals. Topics will include; respiration, heart sounds, temperature, and blood pressure. Hands on practical experience with biomedical instrumentation will be included.

title: Biomaterials
description: Introduction to the role of materials used in medicine such as polymers, metals, ceramics, hydrogels, and smart polymers. Discussions of key biological principals involved with using materials in humans will include cell adhesion, extracellular matrix, cell-biomaterial interactions, inflammation and rejection.

title: Medical Imaging
description: Introduction to the engineering, scientific principles and computation associated with medical imaging technologies including; X-ray, computed tomographic, ultrasound, magnetic resonance, and nuclear imaging. A brief overview of optical technologies and biophotonic approaches will be covered.

title: Directed Studies
description: This course is designed to allow a single student or a group of students to pursue their educational goals and interests under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: This course provides junior-level students with research opportunities in Biomedical Engineering.

title: Biomechanics
description: Statics and dynamics principles applied to the human body to determine forces provided by muscles and impacts on joints. Topics include statics, dynamics, inverse dynamics, fluid dynamics, materials properties of the body and prosthesis design. Labs include anthropometric measurements, analysis of human movement, and measurements of properties of biological materials.

title: Cell and Tissue Engineering w/lab
description: Introduction to the principles and techniques of cell and tissue engineering. Labs will be focused on growing and manipulating human cell lines and conducting a range of cell biology assays. Experiments on creating 3D engineered scaffolds and associated assays will be conducted.

title: Medical and Implantable Devices w/lab
description: Topics will include the theory and applications of therapeutic implantable devices such as pacemakers, defibrillators, artificial skin, and drug delivery systems. Discussion of the role of host reaction to biomaterials, inflammation, and wound healing. Biological testing of devices and prostheses; medical product development, quality assurance for medical products; FDA and ISO requirements for medical devices will also be included.

title: Biological Systems Analysis
description: Computational and mathematical modeling of biological processes (system dynamics and frequency-domain analysis). Topics covered will include population models, metabolic networks, biological oscillation, and dynamics of infectious diseases.

title: BME Capstone I
description: First of a two-course capstone sequence. Hands-on, project-based experiences including engineering design, problem solving and Computer Aided Design (CAD).

title: BME Capstone II
description: Second of a two-course capstone sequence. Conclusion of student-designed projects accompanied by preparation of multiple technical documents.

title: Collaborative Internship
description: This course provides students an opportunity to participate in collaborative projects that are performed on campus under the supervision of a faculty member.

title: Off-campus Biomedical Engineering Internship
description: Off-campus internship with a regional company or other partner, including professional development training and reflection.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: This course provides senior-level students with research opportunities in Biomedical Engineering.

title: Introduction to the World of Business
description: An introduction to the business organization, and the roles and functions performed by those who work within the business.

title: Essentials of Accounting
description: This course teaches students how to interpret basic financial statements. Students learn the parts of annual reports issued by companies, the differences between balance sheets and income statements, and basic accounting terminology.

title: Business Leadership Institute
description: This course helps students develop the knowledge and competencies necessary to reach their educational potential. Topics include leadership, teamwork, goal setting, time management, communication skills (interpersonal, oral, and written), social responsibility, globalization, and diversity.

title: Essentials of Human Resource Management
description: This course examines policies and practices relating to recruitment, selection, training and development, performance appraisal, reward systems, and employee relations. This course is for non-business students seeking a business elective or the business administration certificate.

title: Essentials of Personal Finance
description: This course teaches how to manage your finances. You learn how to track spending, set up a budget, avoid hidden costs in transactions, and invest. You learn personal finance terminology used in the financial press.

title: Essentials of Marketing
description: This course provides students with a basic understanding of marketing. Students learn the role of product, price, promotion, and distribution in the marketing process and explore how marketing impacts their chosen field.

title: Essentials of Management
description: This course explores basic managerial functions such as planning, organizing, staffing, directing, and controlling. Topics include the application of management principles to include aspect of authority, departmentalization, line and staff relations, and comparative management.

title: Essentials of Business Communication
description: This course introduces students to the basics of business communication. Students learn to determine the most effective means to communicate the plan, revise, and polish communication for business.

title: Essentials of Information Systems
description: This course offers a focus on personal information systems to enhance your productivity in today's digital environment. It emphasizes computer use, tool integration, and support of business processes.

title: Essentials of Business Law
description: This course examines the business legal environment, including sources of law and forms of business ownership. You will learn basic principles of agency, contract law, warranty and commercial paper.

title: Essentials of Investing
description: This course teaches the basics of financial investments. You will learn to identify investments and when and how much to invest. Topics include real estate, financial instruments, and insurance annuities. You will learn basic investment terminology.

title: Essentials of Leadership
description: This course provides an overview of the complexities of practicing leadership and offers students concrete strategies for developing their leadership skills. Students will apply these fundamental leadership principles to their own lives through self-assessment and reflection.

title: Foundations for Success in Business
description: Introduction to business and the integration of functional areas within business. Individual and group work investigating foundations of business success. Introduction to College of Business learning goals of teamwork, communication, and problem solving.

title: Special Topics
description: Selected topics of special interest to students and faculty, not normally covered in regular classes.

title: Business Statistics
description: Probability and statistics applied to business problems; frequency and probability distributions, mean and variance, normal distribution, hypothesis testing, linear regression and correlation.

title: Cultural Competence for Today’s Workplace
description: Develops the awareness and sensitivity of students to issues of race, religion, culture, age, gender, sexual orientation, and disabilities in the workplace.

title: Internship Program I
description: Students gain on-the-job experience in business communication in a supervised professional setting.

title: Independent Study Projects
description: Lab/Studio Hours: 0

title: Experiential Applications in Business Administration: Turning Ideas into Impact
description: This course invites students to explore the intersection of their secondary area of concentration with their business administration studies. Through applied projects, case analyses, and reflective practice, students will examine how their secondary area contributes to business problem-solving, innovation, and strategic thinking. Students will work individually and in teams to design and present integrative solutions to real-world business challenges, drawing on both their business core and secondary area expertise. The course emphasizes professional communication, collaboration, and career readiness. The course culminates in a student-directed project that connects their business administration degree to their personal and professional goals.

title: Selected Topics in Business
description: Small groups will study topics selected because of a special interest on the part of students or faculty.

title: Directed Projects
description: Internship experiences in business situations relevant to student's major field of study; under supervision of faculty member.

title: Internship Program II
description: Students gain on-the-job experience in business communication in a supervised professional setting.

title: Independent Study Projects
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Business English and Document Editing
description: Professional written communication must be clear and credible. This introductory course is designed for students who are interested in writing in business settings. The course will include instruction in standard Business English (i.e., spelling, grammar, mechanics, and punctuation); proofreading and editing techniques for business documents; use of style guides; and business document formatting. Through class exercises, individual writing assignments, tutorials, and teamwork, students will improve writing and editing skills to produce more focused, polished, and effective business documents.

title: Business Communication: Writing, Speaking, and Collaborating
description: Develop essential business communication skills, focusing on written communication, oral communication, and collaboration. Topics include workplace communication, message creation, emerging technologies, presentation techniques, and virtual and in-person collaboration. Engage in practical exercises, case studies, and projects to enhance clarity, coherence, and professionalism in messaging for various business audiences and contexts.

title: Business Communication: Persuasive Messaging, Conversations, and Professional Development
description: This course builds essential business communication skills across written, oral, visual, and digital formats. You will leverage foundational communication strategies and communication analysis to communicate in a variety of business contexts, including delivering bad-news messages, crafting persuasive messages, writing and presenting reports and visuals, and communicating in meetings. You will also develop your professional image and prepare for an employment search.

title: Technology for Business Communication
description: Apply current and emerging technologies and applications in order to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of business communication in an organization. Discuss theoretical and practical aspects of business communication technologies to select, use, manage, and problem solve using a range of technologies to process, convey, and present information in business contexts.

title: Advanced Business Writing
description: Further develop your writing skills by creating formal business reports, news releases, instructions, and brochures. Explore writing for print vs. electronic environments.

title: Advanced Business Presentations
description: Theory and practice in making presentations in a variety of professional settings using a variety of support media. Student presentations are evaluated.

title: Communicating in Workplace Relationships
description: This course is designed to enhance students' understanding of the multiple workplace relationships employees may experience in their organization and to develop the business communication skillset necessary to navigate these relationships. Particular emphasis is placed on experiential learning to improve students’ ability to communicate successfully in workplace relationships. Students will author a business communication case study and lead peers through a discussion of the case, conduct informational interviews with full-time working adults on their experiences in workplace relationships and provide an analysis of those experiences, use articles to generate recommendations on how to communicate competently in workplace relationships, and develop and facilitate a training workshop designed to improve some aspect of workplace relationships.

title: Theoretical Approaches to Business Communication
description: Students explore theoretical lenses and common methodologies for analyzing contemporary business communication genres. Particular focus is on the application of theoretical approaches and frameworks commonly used in the rhetorical tradition to critique an organization's messaging and create effective business messages.

title: Co-Creating with AI for Business Communication
description: Explore how artificial intelligence is transforming the way we write, present, and collaborate in professional settings. In this course, students explore a variety of AI tools and workplace scenarios to strengthen rhetorical awareness and business communication skills. They learn to analyze audience, purpose, and context while co-creating with AI to craft messages that are clear, concise, and professional. Through hands-on projects, students create advanced professional communications while identifying the benefits and limitations of AI in business contexts. Students learn to collaborate with AI in ways that infuse human creativity, discernment, and rhetorical awareness in business communication.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Data Visualization for Business Communication
description: Data-driven organizations seek business professionals who can expertly communicate information. This course draws from best practices and research in business communication, graphic design, information literacy, cognitive science, and visual communication to prepare students to effectively incorporate data visualizations in their business communication efforts. Conceptual knowledge and practical application will enable students to grow in their data literacy.

title: Advanced Business Communication
description: An in-depth application of business communication skills. As internal communication specialists, students work in teams to prepare, organize, and deliver business communication training seminars. The course format is an applied business communication context.

title: Internship in Business Communication
description: Students gain on-the-job experience in business communication in a supervised professional setting.

title: Selected Topics in Business Communication
description: Selected topics in business communication chosen because of a special interest on the part of students or faculty.

title: Directed Studies
description: Permits a student or group of students to pursue a defined problem in an intensive way under the direction of departmental faculty members.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Legal and Regulatory Environment
description: This course considers legal and ethical issues affecting American business. Students will learn to recognize, prevent, and manage related risks in the legal and regulatory environment in which businesses operate. Students are introduced to the U.S. court system, alternative dispute resolution techniques, and administrative law. Sustainability of business practices, social responsibility, and rights and duties are emphasized through discussion of topics such as environmental law, employment discrimination, torts, agency principles, and related constitutional law principles.

title: Business Law
description: Law of contracts, sales, negotiable instruments, secured transactions, suretyship, bankruptcy, accountant's legal liability, personal property, bailments and documents of title, real estate, wills, trusts, and insurance.

title: International Business Law
description: A study of private and public law as it affects U.S. companies dealing in international business.

title: Sustainability and Business Law
description: This course examines legal implications at the intersection of sustainability and business law. It covers how sustainability relates to areas like corporate governance, ethics, contracts, employment, torts, criminal law, commercial regulations, consumer law, international law, tax, and intellectual property.
