description: Theories of language acquisition and learning, methodological trends, review of current research, guidelines for teaching the skills and culture.

title: Technology for Learning
description: This course will explore current and emerging technologies that are pertinent to K-12 classrooms and education professionals. Students will develop knowledge about educational technologies and refine the skills needed to equitably incorporate these technologies into K-12 classrooms.

title: Literature for Adolescents
description: Study of literature for young people--middle and high school ages--with emphasis on trends and issues, development of the adolescent novel, genres, author profiles, and reading promotional tools.

title: Social Foundations: Human Relations
description: Students will examine cultural contributions of racial, ethnic, cultural, gender, and economic groups. They will also examine forces of discrimination and racism on individuals, society, and education and analyze practices that promote dignity, social justice, and equality.

title: Instructional Internship in Education for Equity and Justice
description: Selected student develops professional knowledge and skills by engaging in professional reading and discussion during three seminar meetings, providing assistance to faculty member, enhancing classroom teaching and learning for enrolled students, locating and/or developing instructional resources, and other associated duties as assigned by faculty mentor. Plans for internship are based on a student-faculty contract. Three hours of participation per week per credit.

title: Education and Popular Culture
description: This course exposes students to the various and often problematic ways that education is represented in popular culture, using feminist, critical race, and cultural studies perspectives to analyze representations of education and youth in popular culture.

title: Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages
description: Designed to help school teachers and others recognize the language difficulties of non-native speakers and teach effectively the sounds, basic structures, and vocabulary of English. Stresses development and use of practical techniques and materials based on second-language acquisition principles.

title: Methods of Teaching Content-Based EL
description: EL, bilingual education, and content area teachers learn to modify course materials, instructional strategies and assessments so that English learners can engage in course content while simultaneously developing their new language. Student completes approximately 40 hours of field experience.

title: Methods of Bilingual/Bicultural Content-Based Instruction and Curriculum Design
description: The course prepares teacher candidates to work effectively with linguistically diverse students across all content areas and to develop their students' language proficiency as needed for school success. In this course, students will study what it means --linguistically, cognitively, socially, and psychologically –to be a bilingual child. The course will be taught in Spanish and English and will focus on specific instructional strategies for bilingual children, various program models, and methods of assessment for bilingual learners in U.S. public schools.

title: World Language Methods for Early and Middle Childhood
description: Principles and practices in teaching world languages in elementary and middle schools. Preparation for teaching in bilingual education programs. This course also includes a 10-hour practicum experience at a local elementary school.

title: Internship Teaching in K-12 Education
description: The intern is a paid contractual member of a school staff, spending the entire session in their assigned role and following the calendar of their school.

title: Student Teaching in K-12 Education
description: The student teaches full-time for one quarter, following the calendar determined by the Teacher Education Program.

title: Middle Grades Literacy and Assessment
description: Preservice teachers will examine the context and content of a middle grades English Language Arts classroom and curriculum with an emphasis on the process and skills needed for adolescent literacy development. Preservice teachers will learn how to create inquiry-driven literacy curriculum grounded in equitable and developmentally appropriate ELA pedagogies.

title: Student Teaching in Special Situations
description: Intended for students currently enrolled in a teacher licensure program or individuals who are currently licensed educators wishing to add an additional area of licensure. Student teaching experience arranged according to needs of additional licensure.

title: Historical, Legal, and Philosophical Foundations of Education
description: The application of historical, legal, and philosophical principles to classroom operations. Includes the development of a personal philosophy of education.

title: International Experience in Education
description: Allows students to study some aspects of education in another country. Experiences may involve teaching, survey of curriculum, methodology, trends and/or study tour of education facilities. Experience must be approved by department chair.

title: Directed Studies in Education
description: Group study, under the immediate direction of departmental faculty, of a problem not covered in existing courses.

title: Field Experience Seminar
description: A capstone seminar designed to provide experiences in which students refine and demonstrate competence in instructional planning, instructional delivery, and other collaborative and professional practices while meeting the needs of a diverse student population. Taken in conjunction with student teaching/internship. Preparation for the materials and competencies required for the job search is highlighted.

title: Independent Study
description: Study of an education issue under the supervision of Education for Equity and Justice faculty.

title: Social Foundations: Human Relations
description: Students will examine cultural contributions of racial, ethnic, cultural, gender, and economic groups. They will also examine forces of discrimination and racism on individuals, society, and education and analyze practices that promote dignity, social justice, and equality.

title: Education and Popular Culture
description: This course exposes students to the various and often problematic ways that education is represented in popular culture, using feminist, critical race, and cultural studies perspectives to analyze representations of education and youth in popular culture.

title: Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages
description: Designed to help school teachers and others recognize the language difficulties of non-native speakers and teach effectively the sounds, basic structures, and vocabulary of English. Stresses development and use of practical techniques and materials based on second-language acquisition principles.

title: Methods of Teaching Content-Based EL
description: Future and current teachers of English Learners, bilingual educators, and content-area teachers learn how to modify mainstream course materials and instructional strategies so that English Learners can engage in course content while simultaneously developing their new language.

title: Methods of Bilingual/Bicultural Content-Based Instruction and Curriculum Design
description: The course prepares teacher candidates to work effectively with linguistically diverse students across all content areas and to develop their students' language proficiency as needed for school success. In this course, students will study what it means - linguistically, cognitively, socially, and psychologically - to be a bilingual child. The course will be taught in Spanish and English and will focus on specific instructional strategies for bilingual children, various program models, and methods of assessment for bilingual learners in U.S. public schools.

title: Middle Grades Literacy and Assessment
description: Preservice teachers will examine the context and content of a middle grades English Language Arts classroom and curriculum with an emphasis on the process and skills needed for adolescent literacy development. Preservice teachers will learn how to create inquiry-driven literacy curriculum grounded in equitable and developmentally appropriate ELA pedagogies.

title: Historical, Legal, and Philosophical Foundations of Education
description: The application of historical, legal, and philosophical principles to classroom operations. Includes the development of a personal philosophy of education.

title: International Experience in Education
description: Allows students to study some aspects of education in another country. Experiences may involve teaching, survey of curriculum, methodology, trends and/or study tour of education facilities. Experience must be approved by department chair.

title: Directed Studies in Education
description: Group study, under the immediate direction of departmental faculty, of a problem not covered in existing courses.

title: Field Experience Seminar
description: A capstone seminar designed to provide experiences in which students refine and demonstrate competence in instructional planning, instructional delivery, and other collaborative and professional practices while meeting the needs of a diverse student population. Taken in conjunction with student teaching/internship. Preparation for the materials and competencies required for the job search is highlighted.

title: Literacy Assessment
description: Teachers will examine assessment methods for reading and writing, conduct assessments, and analyze data to inform instructional decisions. Issues of literacy assessment will be explored. Work outside of class with a student is required.

title: Student Development and Learning
description: The study of contemporary theories and principles of development, learning and motivation as they relate to educational services. Development issues including: physical, intellectual, emotional, social and moral perspectives will be used to create, modify or change learning expectations in the school setting.

title: Social Foundations of Education
description: The purpose of this course is to help students develop into educators who critically examine the political, social, economic, ideological, and cultural landscape in which they will teach.

title: Cultural and Social Foundations of Learning
description: Designed to provide students the knowledge, skills and dispositions needed to help all students learn more effectively in the school setting. Philosophical underpinnings will provide the basis for learning across the behaviorist to constructivist continuum.

title: Teacher Reflection and Evaluation
description: Designed to assist educators in examining their teaching through reflective practice. Participants will explore and apply multiple ways of reflecting and evaluating their teaching from peer assistance to self-evaluations, student interviews, and teaching portfolios.

title: Mathematics Curriculum, Instruction, and Evaluation in Grades 1-12
description: A survey of current trends and issues related to mathematics teaching and learning in elementary through high school. Contemporary instructional procedures, curricula and assessment methods will be examined. Technology integration will be infused.

title: Issues of Critical Literacy in Grades K-12
description: A major thrust of this course will be on the social, cultural, and political dimensions of literacy education in grades K-12.

title: Comparative Education
description: Theory, structure, and organization of education. Examines education in selected countries of Europe, South America, Near East, Middle East, and Far East.

title: Administration and Leadership in Reading Instruction
description: Provides the prospective Reading Specialist with coaching models, study of reading program reform and home, school and community partnerships. Examines leadership strategies to administer and promote the K-12 district reading instruction and assessment program.

title: Social Studies Curriculum and Instruction in Grades 1-12
description: Analysis of trends, issues, materials, and practices in Social Studies education.

title: Science Curriculum and Instruction in Grades 1-12
description: Advanced study of contemporary curriculum models and strategies for teaching science. Investigates current issues affecting 1-12 science teaching.

title: Current Trends and Issues in Curriculum 1-12
description: Organizing elements, current issues, innovations, and trends in curriculum for grades 1-12.

title: Curricular Design and Innovation
description: This course investigates curricular models aligned with state and national standards, with a focus on how curricular design promotes learning and innovation in the classroom setting.

title: Introduction to Action Research
description: Students will create and conduct an action research project in their own classrooms using appropriate research methodologies.

title: Advanced Topics in Teaching
description: A collection of one-credit courses, varying in scope and in degree of emphasis on educational issues and/or practices, designed to provide continued study of topics related to professional issues and professional performance.

title: Instructional Supervision Theory
description: A theory based course designed to develop the competence of those preparing to supervise teacher education students. Fees may be waived if the cooperating teacher, team leader, or administrator is serving the UW-Eau Claire teacher education program.

title: Advanced Practica and Topics in Supervision
description: A practicum based course designed to enhance and refine the supervisory skills of cooperating teachers supervising teacher education students. Fees may be waived if the cooperating teacher is serving the UW-Eau Claire teacher education program.

title: Language Arts Curriculum and Instruction in Grades 1-8
description: Materials, procedures, and organization of elementary and middle school language arts. For teachers, supervisors, and administrators in elementary and middle schools.

title: Teaching All Students: Equity, Diversity and Inclusivity
description: Beginning with the end-in-mind, this course addresses issues of diversity, equity and inclusivity so that all students become successful learners.

title: Facilitating Literacy Across the Curriculum
description: Rationale and methods for determining and meeting the needs and abilities of middle level, secondary, and post-secondary students to read text in content areas.

title: Collaborative Leadership: Building Effective Relationships
description: Designed to help students build and align their knowledge, skills and dispositions as Teacher and Collaborative Leader in order to act in concert with colleagues, administrators, students and their parents.

title: Administrative Leadership
description: A study of selected theories and research relating to individual and group behaviors in K-12 educational organizations with emphasis on the characteristics of educational leadership including participatory management, long range strategic planning and change agent processes. Particular emphasis is given to human relations skills as well as oral and written communication skills needed by public school leaders.

title: Seminar in Reading
description: Content determined by both professor and students. Multiple topics for consideration presented by instructor. Each class member is required to be familiar with all topics and to investigate one in considerable depth.

title: School Law
description: Study of the legal system, Wisconsin Statutes relating to schools, federally mandated programs, teacher and student freedoms and responsibilities, and case law related to the foregoing.

title: Designing a Learning Environment
description: This course addresses issues of risk, power, student needs, and classroom management within the framework of building an effective classroom community. The theories of the responsive classroom development and design will be explored.

title: Seminar in Elementary and Middle Level Education
description: An intensive study of selected topics in elementary and middle level education. The exact topics to be studied will vary according to the interests of the professor and the seminar participants.

title: Evaluation, Assessment and Data-Based Decision Making
description: Designed to help students create, analyze and use reliable and valid assessments so that they can use that data for instructional decision-making in both the classroom and their school.

title: Teaching Adult Learners
description: Examines the nature and needs of adult learners, approaches to adult learning programs, methods for instructing and evaluating adult learners, as well as trends and projections in adult education.

title: Program Evaluation
description: The development of competency in designing, implementing, reporting, and assessing a plan to evaluate academic and nonacademic educational programs.

title: Introduction to Educational Inquiry
description: Study of educational inquiry and research methodologies. Intended to increase student's ability to critically read and synthesize the products of educational inquiry, to conceptualize and conduct a clear, disciplined inquiry into a topic.

title: Scholarly Paper
description: Selection and implementation of the scholarship/research process with guidance from an Education Studies graduate adviser. Culminates in a paper and an oral presentation.

title: Directed Studies
description: A group may investigate common problems.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Thesis
description: A description of acceptable topics and the precise nature of the thesis requirement is provided in the departmental program descriptions.

title: The Study of Language
description: This course is a non-technical introduction to the study of human language and its role in human interaction. Students survey essential topics related to all aspects of language as a social, biological, psychological, and cultural construct.

title: Introduction to World/Postcolonial Literature
description: Introduction to Eastern and Western literary classics from ancient and/or modern times (excluding British and American), with emphasis on cultural significance. Attention may also be given to origin and development of literary forms, modes, techniques, and conventions.

title: Introduction to Literature
description: Appreciation and analysis of great works of literature in fiction (short story and novel), poetry, and drama, through the application of a range of critical techniques.

title: Introduction to Critical Studies in Film, Television, and Moving-Image Culture
description: Introduction to critical studies in film, television, and moving-image culture. Includes concepts such as representation and ideology; race, class, and gender; economics and history; aesthetics and politics; and genre and form. Variable focuses and emphases.

title: Introduction to Critical Studies
description: Students learn methods for understanding how texts construct meaning and shape frameworks of power that mediate human relationships.

title: Histories and Theories of Rhetoric
description: Introduction to the field of rhetoric through historical, contemporary, and theoretical perspectives.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing
description: Introduction to creative writing with opportunities for the student to create and evaluate several forms of verbal art (poetry, fiction and/or nonfiction).

title: Introductory English Linguistics
description: Introduction to the nature and structure of human language with an emphasis on the English language. Survey topics include phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, pragmatics, history of the English language, regional and social dialects, language acquisition.

title: Survey of World/Postcolonial Literature
description: This course provides a comparative survey of literature from a minimum of four western (excluding British and North American) and non-western cultures over two or more centuries from the fourteenth century onward.

title: American Literature to 1865
description: A survey of literature created in America from the pre-Columbian period to the end of the Civil War.

title: American Literature from 1865-1945
description: Poetry, fiction, nonfiction, drama, and folktales of the late nineteenth century and the first half of the twentieth, from Chopin, Gilman and Twain to Eliot, Faulkner, O'Neill, and Hurston.

title: American Literature Since 1945
description: Poetry, fiction, nonfiction, and drama since 1945, including such authors as Miller, Lowell, Didion, and Warren.

title: Perspectives on Pre-1790 British Literature
description: Introductory-level study and appreciation of pre-1790 British literatures situated across a major shift of at least two literary periods (e.g., before and after the Norman Conquest, medieval and early modern theatricality or Arthurian myths).

title: Representative Shakespeare
description: Analysis of a representative group of texts (plays and verse) with attention to genre, historical contexts, or theoretical approaches. Does not duplicate English 357.

title: Perspectives on British Literature After 1790
description: This course investigates a specific topic or group of British authors situated across a major shift of at least two literary periods (e.g., Romanticism to Victorianism) ranging from 1790 to the present.

title: Survey of American Ethnic Literature
description: Focuses on literary techniques/forms that represent experiences of at least one particular American ethnic group. Analyzes relationships between an ethnic culture and the dominant culture, emphasizing the intersection in literature of ethnic/dominant traditions, histories, cultural values.

title: Perspectives in Popular Texts
description: Perspectives on a theme, issue, topic, genre, major author, or movement in mass culture as presented in popular texts.

title: Creative and Narrative Nonfiction
description: Survey of nonfiction works (reportage, autobiography, travel, and nature writing) which combines factual reporting with literary techniques to reveal American society.

title: The Short Story
description: Analysis and discussion of ideas and literary techniques found in the short story with attention to such writers as Chekhov, Dinesen, Borges, Welty.

title: The Novel
description: Analysis and discussion of ideas and literary techniques found in the novel with attention to such writers as Austen, Hawthorne, Twain, Joyce, Dostoevsky, Woolf, Heller.

title: Poetry
description: Analysis and discussion of ideas and literary techniques found in poetry with attention to such writers as Shakespeare, Frost, Yeats, Dickinson, Whitman, Rich, Plath, Heaney.

title: Drama
description: Analysis and discussion of ideas, literary techniques, and conventions found in drama. Selections will normally range from classical to contemporary and include examples from diverse cultures.

title: Young Adult Literature
description: This course is designed to introduce various genres of young adult literature, along with critical theories that can help us to read these texts with a lens for how issues of perspective, power, and privilege are woven into narratives. In this course we will consider what constitutes young adult literature, why this genre appeals to readers who may no longer consider themselves “young,” and how we might engage with this content in ways that bring meaning to our own lived experiences.

title: Critical Perspectives on Film, Television, and Moving-Image Culture
description: Engagement with critical perspectives on film, television, and/or moving image culture. Students will analyze films, TV shows, and/or moving-image texts using a variety of critical methods and approaches.

title: Introduction to Theory and Criticism
description: Introduction to a range of positions concerning what, how, and why we read within English Studies. Students will develop their ability to recognize, explain, and argue for their own positions in relation to these issues.

title: Perspectives on Women's Literature
description: Analyzes representative texts by women. Attention given to historical and critical/theoretical questions relevant to understanding women's achievements in such areas as writing for literary fame, artistic creation, religious expression, political resistance, and economic survival.

title: Teaching Writing in the Elementary and Middle School
description: Writing intensive course focused on writing theory and pedagogy in the elementary and middle school. Students will develop their writing skills as well as learn to support writing development of children in a writing workshop.

title: Editing and Publications Management
description: Copyediting and substantive editing of text and art. Addresses use of style guides and stylesheets, editor/author rapport, document design, indexing, and the publication process. Flexible strategies that consider conventions, cognition, and rhetorical situation.

title: Intermediate Poetry Writing
description: The study, creation, and evaluation of poetry for the intermediate writing student.

title: Intermediate Fiction/Nonfiction Writing
description: The study, creation, and evaluation of fiction and nonfiction for the intermediate writing student.

title: Science Writing
description: Develop students' abilities to respond to rhetorical situations informed by scientific evidence and create documents that respond effectively to these situations.

title: Technical Writing
description: Students analyze rhetorical situations and user needs, determine optimal formats, and produce effective technical documents for intended audiences.

title: Cultural Rhetorics
description: Topics in rhetorical action of cultural groups. Explores relationships among rhetoric, identity, ideology, power, privilege, and social change.

title: Visual Rhetorics of Science, Technology, and Culture
description: Examines texts as comprised of both verbal and visual elements that work together to make meaning, create knowledge, and persuade audiences.

title: Rhetorics of Social Justice
description: Develops students’ understanding of inequitable power systems to enable students to produce ethical and fitting rhetorical responses in the public sphere. Sections will vary in thematic approach.

title: Introduction to English Education Methods
description: An introduction to the theories and methods of teaching English language arts in middle and high school classrooms.

title: Topics in the Structure of English
description: Explores the inner workings of the English language. Specific course offerings will involve one or more areas of language structure: phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, pragmatics.

title: Topics in Language in Society
description: Explores issues of language in society from a linguistic perspective. Topics may include the history of the English language, language attitudes, language change, language variation, and others.

title: Topics in World/Postcolonial Literature
description: A thematic analysis of issues pertaining to the study of world (excluding British and American) and/or postcolonial literatures.

title: Women in African Literature
description: Using cultural and historical perspectives, the course interprets images of women in African literature, emphasizing how the experiences of phases of colonialism have continued to transform women's images, roles, and prospects in society.

title: Topics in American Literature to 1865
description: This focused study of American literature may include works from the pre-European contact period through 1865. A major author, genre, theme, or movement may be the subject of this course.

title: Topics in American Literature: 1865 - Present
description: A focused study of American literature between the end of Civil War and the present. A major author, genre, theme, or movement related to this period may be the subject.

title: Chaucer and His Age
description: Literature and language of Geoffrey Chaucer in his literary and social context; readings from his Canterbury Tales and other writings, and from comparable works by his contemporaries and/or imitators through ca. 1450.

title: Topics in British Literature Before 1790
description: This course will investigate a specific literary period, topic, or group of authors in British literature before 1790.

title: Shakespearean Topics
description: Analysis of a selected group of plays or poems in terms of a specific thematic or theoretical topic.

title: Topics in British Literature After 1790
description: This course will investigate a specific literary period, topic, or group of British authors within the period ranging from 1790 to the present.

title: Studies in Transatlantic Romanticism
description: Examines Romanticism as an Anglo-American literary dialogue concerned with topics such as abolition of slavery, environmental preservation, women's rights, etc. Explores how the successes and failures of political, poetical, and industrial revolutions shaped Romantic literature.

title: Topics in American Ethnic Literature
description: This literature course explores the literary perspectives and cultural lives embodied by U.S. ethnic communities, particularly African American, Asian American, American Indian, and Hispanic/Latin American communities. Individual sections explore ethnic literature produced by an author, literary period, genre, or theme.

title: Topics in Popular Culture
description: Thoughtful, critical, and in-depth study of a theme, issue, topic, genre, major author, or movement in mass culture as presented in popular texts and related critical discussions.

title: Critical Studies in Film and Culture
description: Critical exploration of topics in film and culture. Variable focus by offering. Recommended for English majors/minors and others with significant interest in film, video, and moving-image culture or in cinema and media history.

title: Studies in Theory and Criticism
description: Study and analysis of selected theory and/or criticism. Specific offerings under this course title will vary, focusing on author, theme, period, genre.

title: Travel Seminar
description: Study before, during, or after travel to a region associated with a writer or writers, e.g. Thomas Hardy's Wessex. Emphasizes visits to libraries, museums, cities, countryside, and special sites associated with the literature.

title: Academic Apprenticeship in English
description: Qualified students, supervised by English faculty, facilitate teaching and learning in a specific course. Students will enhance their knowledge of the discipline and their ability to communicate this understanding to their peers.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in language or literature.

title: Studies in Women's Literature
description: Study of a particular problem, genre, or literary period within that literature written by or about women, such as "Our Literary Foremothers" or "Jane Eyre's Literary Daughters".

title: Writing Center Theory & Practice
description: Introduction to Writing Center Theory and its application to tutoring college writing in a variety of disciplines. Students tutor three hours per week in the Center for Writing Excellence. This course is open to non-majors.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Grant Proposal Writing
description: Students will gain knowledge of various grant proposal forms (government, corporate, foundation), with emphasis on conceptualizing, developing, and writing proposals for real clients. Each student will complete an individual and a collaborative proposal.

title: Creative Writing Workshop - Poetry
description: Special emphasis on class discussion and personal conferences for advanced writers of poetry.

title: Creative Writing Workshop - Fiction
description: Special emphasis on class discussion and personal conferences for advanced writers of fiction.

title: Creative Writing Workshop - Nonfiction
description: Writing and reading nonfiction, with an emphasis on producing an essay of publishable quality for a general, literate audience.

title: Prose Writing Workshop-Topics
description: Reading and writing in one or more prose genres. Topics may include: writing for children, historical fiction, mystery, young adult.

title: Seminar in English Education Methods
description: Seminar focused on constructivist theories of literacy teaching and the application of those theories in middle and high school classrooms.

title: Seminar in Linguistic Research
description: Theory and practice of linguistic research, on a general theme designated by the instructor. Students design and carry out research projects, choosing language problems and research methodology suggested by the theme.

title: Seminar in World/Postcolonial Literature
description: In-depth study of specialized topics in world/postcolonial literature such as Postcolonial Feminism or Magic Realism in the Latin American Novel.

title: Seminar in American Literature Before 1865
description: Advanced study of specialized topics in American literature before 1865, including theoretical (e.g., Racial Pseudoscience in the American Novel), generic (e.g., The Antebellum Romance), and special themes (e.g., The Transcendentalists and Social Reform).

title: Seminar in American Literature Since 1865
description: Advanced study of selected topics in American literature since 1865, including genres or subgenres, theoretical perspectives, ethnic emphases, special themes, and paired or individual authors.

title: Seminar in Early British Literature
description: In-depth exploration of selected topics in British Literature before 1790, including theoretical (e.g., Feminism and Medieval Literature), generic (e.g., Medieval Drama, Early Modern Poetry, Romance), and special themes (e.g., Death in Early British Literature).

title: Seminar in Rhetorics of Science, Technology, and Culture
description: Course provides capstone experience in theory and praxis of rhetorics in science, technology, and/or culture. Content of individual course iterations will vary depending on instructor expertise/interests, but will balance rhetorical analysis with textual production.

title: Seminar in British Literature After 1790
description: Advanced study of specialized topics in British literature after 1790, including theoretical (e.g., intersection of Feminism and British Modernism), generic (e.g., Romantic Poetry, Modern Drama, Postmodern Novel), special themes (e.g., Booker Novels), and author studies (e.g., Dickens and Collins).

title: Seminar in American Ethnic Literature
description: Advanced study of topics and texts authored by American Ethnic writers. Topics may include theoretical, political, aesthetic, and cultural issues reflecting the historical and cultural forces that have shaped the literary production of authors from a particular American ethnic tradition.

title: English Capstone
description: Students elect either a one-credit Capstone course coincident with a 400-level seminar, completing a combined "senior thesis" project; or a three-credit Capstone course, completing the senior project independent of any specific seminar.

title: Seminar in Film, Video, and Moving-Image Culture
description: Advanced explorations in film, video, and moving-image culture studies. Variable focus by offering. Seminar topics will often include significant engagement with work in various forms of critical theory.

title: Seminar in Critical Theory
description: In-depth exploration of topics in Critical Theory, including schools (e.g., Feminist Theory), periods (e.g., 20th Century Theory), figures (e.g., Foucauldian Theory), and special themes (e.g., Theorizing Music).

title: Seminar in Women's Literature
description: Advanced study of specialized topics in literature authored by women--e.g., theory (Womanist Writings and Readings), period (Women's WWI Fiction), genre (Feminism in Science Fiction), or theme (Woman's Work: Gender and Class Before 1790).

title: Honors Independent Study
description: Intensive study of a topic resulting in an honors quality scholarly, creative, or other type of project appropriate to English majors fulfilling the English Departmental Honors option.

title: Internship in Writing
description: Experience in a professional organization working as a technical writer, editor, etc. Work will build on and relate to writing skills developed in other courses in writing.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Teaching Writing in the Elementary and Middle School
description: Writing intensive course focused on writing theory and pedagogy in the elementary and middle school. Students will develop their writing skills as well as learn to support writing development of children in a writing workshop.

description: Advanced study of specialized topics in British literature after 1790, including theoretical (e.g., intersection of Feminism and British Modernism), generic (e.g., Romantic Poetry, Modern Drama, Postmodern Novel), special themes (e.g., Booker Novels), and author studies (e.g., Dickens and Collins).

description: Advanced study of topics and texts authored by American Ethnic writers. Topics may include theoretical, political, aesthetic, and cultural issues reflecting the historical and cultural forces that have shaped the literary production of authors from a particular American ethnic tradition.

title: Seminar in Critical Theory
description: In-depth exploration of topics in Critical Theory, including schools (e.g., Feminist Theory), periods (e.g., 20th Century Theory), figures (e.g., Foucauldian Theory), and special themes (e.g., Theorizing Music).

title: Seminar in Women's Literature
description: Advanced study of specialized topics in literature authored by women--e.g., theory (Womanist Writings and Readings), period (Women's WWI Fiction), genre (Feminism in Science Fiction), or theme (Woman's Work: Gender and Class Before 1790).

title: Critical Thinking, Reading, and Writing
description: Introduction to graduate level critical reading and writing, stressing the development of insightful analysis and critical voice supported by an understanding of a variety of critical approaches and research methodologies.

title: Critical Theory and English Studies
description: Seminar examining the function of critical theory in studies of literature, language, and culture, focusing on the place of English Studies within the culture and the relations between critical theory and the disciplines of English.

title: Studies in Writing, Language, or Pedagogy
description: Seminar examining topics of importance in writing, language, or pedagogy (e.g., Approaches to Teaching English, Social Forces and Linguistic Change, or The Nature of Composition Studies: Historical, Institutional, Cultural Forces).

title: Poetry Writing Seminar
description: The study, creation, workshop evaluation and revision of poems.

title: Studies in Scientific and Technical Communication
description: Theory and research on scientific and technical communication. Emphasis on the role of current theory and research in informing best practices in textual and graphic documentation.

title: Studies in World/Postcolonial Literature
description: Theoretically focused seminar on a particular theme, genre, or historical period from a geographical region other than Great Britain or North America (e.g., the European Novel, Literature of the African Diaspora, or Postcolonial Fiction).

title: Studies in North American Literature
description: Theoretically focused seminar on a particular ethnic group, race, culture, or historical period in North America (e.g. Native Voices in American Literature, African American Writing 1830-1900, or American Poetry: Whitman to Frost).

title: Studies in British and Irish Literature
description: Theoretically focused seminar on a particular region, group, or period in Great Britain (e.g. British Women Writers--seventeenth and eighteenth century, Kailyard School of Scottish Fiction, Exile and the Irish Writer, or twentieth century British Poetry).

title: Studies in Figures and Groups
description: Theoretically focused seminar on a particular figure or literary group, including investigation of philosophical and political background, cultural milieu, literary influences, and critical reception (e.g. Shakespeare, Alice Walker, Thomas Pynchon, the Beats, or the Brontes).

title: Studies in Themes and Genres
description: Theoretically focused seminar on a particular theme or genre that extends beyond geographical boundaries and traditional literary periods (e.g., Evolution of the Novel, Nineteenth Century Gothicism, Autobiography in Western Literature, or Our Literary Foremothers).

title: Studies in Theory and Culture
description: Seminar exploring theoretical issues, historical or contemporary, with emphasis on critical application and implication (e.g. Cultural Studies Theory, Literary Post- Modernism, Theories of Degeneration in the British Novel, Film Noir, or Cultural Perspectives on Literacy).

title: Graduate Apprenticeship: Teaching Theory and Practice
description: Students will gain college classroom teaching preparation and experience under the supervision of a graduate faculty member teaching an undergraduate course. In addition, students will read within a select bibliography of texts on pedagogy.

title: Directed Studies
description: Permits a group of students to investigate a common topic under the direction of a department faculty member.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Thesis
description: A description of acceptable topics and the precise nature of the thesis requirement is provided in the departmental program descriptions.

title: Integrated English Language Skills for Non-Native Speakers II
description: This intermediate level multi-skill ESL course is designed to improve listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills as well as grammar, pronunciation, and vocabulary while preparing students for academic learning.

title: Introduction to Academic Reading and Writing
description: This course, which focuses on the processes of reading and writing, will develop the academic writing skills of non-native speakers of English.

title: The Pronunciation of American English
description: Designed for non-native speakers of English. To promote clear pronunciation for effective communication with a focus on comprehensibility to appropriate native-like target pronunciation. This course teaches ESL learners to hear, identify, and clearly articulate the sounds and patterns in spoken English.

title: Oral Presentation Skills for Non-Native Speakers of English
description: This course is intended to equip ESL students with the pragmatic and linguistic tools necessary to communicate more effectively in English. The course is designed to provide them with meaningful oral communication practice and speaking solutions toward the improvement of interaction, presentation, and pronunciation skills.

title: Intermediate Learning to Learn
description: This intermediate-level course focuses on learning skills and strategies, academic expectations, and campus and community resources. Students are matched with international student mentors.

title: Special Topics for ESL Learners
description: In-depth study of a special area in the learning of English as a Second Language.

title: Integrated English Language Skills for Non-Native Speakers III
description: This advanced level multi-skill ESL course is designed to improve listening, speaking, reading, writing, and grammar skills while preparing students for further academic work or a career using English.

title: Advanced Academic Writing
description: This advanced-level course focuses on the processes of reading and writing to prepare non-native speakers of English for success in academic writing in University-level courses.

title: Multicultural Literature for Non-Native Speakers of English
description: Designed to develop analytical and critical thinking skills through the study of multicultural literature.

title: Advanced Pronunciation
description: This course will improve students' ability to hear, identify and clearly articulate the sounds, stress patterns, intonation and rhythm of American English. Listening skills, oral skills, and clear communication with native speakers will be improved.

title: Business English for Non-Native Speakers
description: Designed for non-native English speaking students. Intended to equip English language learners with the English writing, speaking, and listening skills necessary to communicate effectively on the job while building learners' English vocabulary for the business discourse domain.

title: US Culture and Institutions for ESL Students
description: This advanced-level language proficiency course is designed for ESL students to improve listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills while providing an introduction to the institutions and cultures of the U.S.A. and the implications these have on how Americans think and behave both nationally and internationally.

title: Advanced Learning to Learn
description: This course focuses on learning skills and strategies, academic expectations in the U.S., and connecting to campus and community resources. This course is for advanced level English language learners only.

title: Professional English Advanced Special Topics for ESL Learners
description: In-depth study of a special area in the learning of English as a Second Language. This special topics course is for advanced level English language learners only. Topics specified in the Class Schedule.

title: Creativity and the Entrepreneurial Mindset
description: Students will explore the minds of entrepreneurs by participating in experiential learning activities aimed at enhancing their creativity and understanding the cognitive processes essential to the entrepreneurial mindset.

title: Customer Discovery and Business Model Design
description: Students explore entrepreneurship by learning and utilizing a business model framework to guide their exploration and discovery process, toward the creation of a successful startup.

title: New Venture Feasibility
description: Students apply market research techniques to refine their business ideas and evaluate market feasibility. Students learn how to conduct operational and financial analyses in the design of a feasible business model.

title: New Venture Launch
description: Students learn how to launch a new product and explore the range of functional details involved in a new venture start-up. Strategies for growing the business and challenges in management growth are addressed.

title: Internship Program
description: Students will participate in work experiences related to their area of interest.

title: Entrepreneurship Capstone
description: Students will learn and demonstrate how to skillfully prepare, refine, and communicate their business idea in an entrepreneurial environment. They will learn about financing options considered by startup founders. They will deliver their business plan in a formal, end-of- semester presentation to a panel of business professionals.

title: Topics in Entrepreneurship
description: Current issues in entrepreneurship will be the focus of attention. Topic selection will be made by the instructor. Issues will be those facing contemporary entrepreneurship professionals.

title: Introduction to Environmental Health
description: Health-oriented problems in the environment with attention directed to air and water pollution, solid waste, housing, occupational health and safety, food sanitation, animal zoonoses, ecology of health and disease, radiological health, energy, and global environmental health.

title: Special Topics
description: A variable content course consisting of topics of special interest to students and faculty that are not covered in other environmental health courses.

title: Radiation, Air Pollution and Health
description: Examines the fundamentals, human health implications, and safety practices related to ionizing radiation from radioactive materials. Examines the fundamentals, human, and environmental health consequences of air pollution, along with mitigation strategies.

title: Occupational Safety Management
description: Recognition and control of safety hazards in various workplaces, basic safety theory, applicable health and safety regulations, and ethical obligations. Behavioral change techniques. Opportunity for students to research specific workplace settings and issues.

title: Directed Studies
description: For the student who wishes to pursue a defined, organized study of an environmental public health topic under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Student Academic Apprenticeship
description: Experience for qualified students to facilitate teaching and learning in ENPH courses. Students develop and enhance knowledge in ENPH and their abilities to communicate that knowledge to other students.

title: Independent Study
description: An opportunity for students to pursue an environmental research topic of their choice in collaboration with a faculty mentor.

title: Preparation for Practicum
description: An interactive course covering expectations during practicum, presentations on various areas of environmental health practice, opportunities for shadowing, preparation of resumes, practice interviewing.

title: Practicum in Environmental Public Health
description: A full-time supervised 10-week internship experience in an approved governmental agency, industry, or consulting company.

title: Water and Wastewater
description: Investigative procedures, sampling techniques, analysis and treatment of water and wastewater. Emphasis on water pollution, aquatic nuisances, drinking water quality, on-site waste disposal, municipal and industrial wastewater treatment, private wells, and groundwater contamination.

title: Microbial Safety of Food
description: Principles of food microbiology including common foodborne pathogens, strategies to protect the food supply, environmental sanitation, regulations, and HACCP. Environmental sampling of food and milk, common laboratory tests.

title: Hazardous and Solid Waste Management
description: Examination of the generation, use, handling, and storage of solid waste, and of materials posing significant chemical, biological, and radiological risks to health. Several field trips will supplement in-class learning.

title: Fundamentals of Industrial Hygiene
description: The recognition, evaluation, and control of chemical, physical, biological and ergonomic stresses that may cause sickness or impaired health to employees or residents of the community.

title: Vector-Borne Disease Control
description: Vector-borne disease health effects, identification of vectors and pests, and approaches for preventing vector-borne disease globally, nationally and regionally.

title: Environmental Toxicology and Risk Assessment
description: Examination of the principles, theories, and applications of human environmental toxicology through investigation of chemical, cellular, physiological, environmental, and ecological levels. Introduction to contemporary health risk assessment methods.

title: Special Topics
description: A variable content course consisting of advanced topics of special interest to students and faculty that are not covered in other environmental health courses.

title: Directed Studies
description: Permits the student to pursue a defined organized study of a problem in an intensive way under the direction of a department of public health professions staff member.

title: Independent Study
description: An opportunity for senior students to pursue an environmental topic of their choice. A detailed project report will be required.

title: Water and Wastewater
description: Investigative procedures, sampling techniques, analysis and treatment of water and wastewater. Emphasis on water pollution, aquatic nuisances, drinking water quality, on-site waste disposal, municipal and industrial wastewater treatment, private wells, and groundwater contamination.

title: Water and Wastewater
description: Investigative procedures, sampling techniques, analysis and treatment of water and wastewater. Emphasis on water pollution, aquatic nuisances, drinking water quality, on-site waste disposal, municipal and industrial wastewater treatment, private wells, and groundwater contamination.

title: Hazardous and Solid Waste Management
description: Examination of the generation, use, handling, and storage of solid waste, and of materials posing significant chemical, biological, and radiological risks to health. Several field trips will supplement in-class learning.

title: Hazardous and Solid Waste Management
description: Examination of the generation, use, handling, and storage of solid waste, and of materials posing significant chemical, biological, and radiological risks to health. Several field trips will supplement in-class learning.

title: Vector-Borne Disease Control
description: Vector-borne disease health effects, identification of vectors and pests, and approaches for preventing vector-borne disease globally, nationally and regionally.

title: Vector-Borne Disease Control
description: Vector-borne disease health effects, identification of vectors and pests, and approaches for preventing vector-borne disease globally, nationally and regionally.

title: Environmental Toxicology and Risk Assessment
description: Examination of the principles, theories, and applications of human environmental toxicology through investigation of chemical, cellular, physiological, environmental, and ecological levels. Introduction to contemporary health risk assessment methods.

title: Environmental Toxicology and Risk Assessment
description: Examination of the principles, theories, and applications of human environmental toxicology through investigation of chemical, cellular, physiological, environmental, and ecological levels. Introduction to contemporary health risk assessment methods.

title: Foundations of Sustainability
description: Around the world, sustainability has become an organizing principle for organizational change. This course applies different definitions of and frameworks for sustainability as it examines fundamental planetary limits and how to achieve sustainability within them. Labs integrate key course concepts with personal experience to build skills for application to students' personal and professional lives

title: Water Problems, Water Solutions
description: This course introduces students to the political, social, and environmental challenges of water use and management in the United States and worldwide.

title: Special Topics - Environmental Studies
description: A variable content course designed to allow a breadth of exposure to topics in environmental and/or sustainability studies selected because of special interest on the part of students or faculty. May include field trips.

title: Sustainable Cities
description: Using Portland, Oregon, as a case study, the course will explore sustainable cities from the perspective of health, the environment, and social cohesion.

title: Resources & Society: Energy, Food, and Waste
description: An interdisciplinary overview of the systems involved in the production, consumption, and disposal of key resources, with an exploration of more efficient and sustainable alternatives. Specific topics include: energy production and use, the food supply chain, resource extraction, and the waste stream.

title: American Environmental History
description: Examines interactions between human societies and the natural world in what is now the United States. Through readings and several field trips students will learn the essential elements of American Environmental History.

title: U.S. Environmental and Sustainability Policy
description: Students will study U.S. environmental and sustainability law and policy to assess the roles of science, key actors, and values in policymaking.

title: International Environmental Problems and Policy
description: Examination of the threats to the global environment and the response of the international community.

title: Taking the Measure of Sustainability
description: Students will survey a selection of quantitative tools and approaches for evaluating and improving sustainability in a range of contexts including individual choices, corporate strategies, and governmental policies. In a culminating group project, students will gain marketable skills in applying these techniques to measure the carbon footprint of the university or evaluate the sustainability of a local business or non-profit partner.

title: Directed Studies
description: For the student who wishes to pursue a defined, organized study of an interdisciplinary environmental studies topic under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: An opportunity for students to pursue an interdisciplinary environmental studies topic in collaboration with a faculty mentor. A detailed project plan and report will be required.

title: Special Topics - Advanced Environmental Studies
description: A variable content course designed to allow a breadth of study through intensive investigation of a topic in environmental and/or sustainability studies selected because of special interest on the part of students or faculty. May include field trips.

title: Directed Studies - Seniors
description: For the senior student who wishes to pursue a defined, organized study of an interdisciplinary environmental studies topic under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Environmental Studies Internship
description: Appointment to a professional experience in an area of interest related to a student's theme of study, applying the student's understanding of environmental studies.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: An opportunity for senior students to pursue an interdisciplinary environmental studies topic in collaboration with a faculty mentor. A detailed project plan and report will be required.

title: American Environmental History
description: Examines interactions between human societies and the natural world in what is now the United States. Through readings and several field trips students will learn the essential elements of American Environmental History.

title: Topics in Finance
description: Covers a variety of topics to be determined by faculty interest and student demand.

title: Principles of Finance
description: Study of the techniques used to make investment, financing, and dividend decisions in order to maximize the value of the firm to its owners. Topics include financial planning and control, working capital management, capital budgeting, capital structure, cost of capital, dividend policy, and international finance.

title: Financial Markets and Institutions
description: A study of the U.S. financial system. Topics include the Federal Reserve System, term structure of interest rates, financial markets, instruments and financial institutions including regulation of commercial banks.

title: Investments
description: Overview of security markets. Analysis of stocks, bonds, and other securities. Evaluation of risk and return characteristics and market efficiency.

title: International Financial Management
description: An introduction to the international elements of a business's operating environment. Topics include global economic factors, risk management, cost of capital, capital budgeting, and financing in a global environment.

title: Short Term Financial Management
description: Short term financial management includes the management of working capital accounts. Banking relations and performance analysis will complete the tools required for a financial manager.

title: Long Term Financial Management
description: Long term financing and investment issues to include capital budgeting methods, cash flow determination, risk analysis, long term debt, preferred and common stock financing, and leasing.

title: Introduction to Derivative Securities
description: Introduction to options, futures, and forward contracts as financial instruments used to control various types of business risk.

title: Wealth Management
description: Understand the breadth of the individual wealth management process and the wealth management profession.

title: Financial Modeling
description: This course will focus on conceptual applications involved in Corporate Finance. Content is structured around career expectations in corporate finance. This is where the Financial Analyst is part of the management team responsible for managing financial actions of one particular company or organization. This course presents the theory and practice of corporate financial management. Special emphasis will be on emphasizing computer-based modelling. Spreadsheets will be used to analyze financial and non-financial information to make business decisions.

title: Investment Strategy
description: The course provides students the opportunity to obtain experience in the process of managing an investment portfolio.

title: Topics in Finance
description: Covers a variety of topics to be determined by faculty interest and student demand.

title: Independent Study Projects
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Corporate Valuation
description: Applications of financial management theories and tools to decisions in capital budgeting, financial structure, current asset management, etc. Application is done through use of case and computer analysis.

title: Portfolio Management
description: Students will create and manage mock portfolios to learn asset allocation, portfolio construction, and management methods. Other topics include developing investment policies, the psychology of portfolio choices, ethics, and standards of practice in portfolio management.

title: Topics in Finance
description: Covers a variety of advanced topics to be determined by faculty interest and student demand.

title: Advanced Wealth Management
description: Advanced issues in financial planning are analyzed by means of case studies, readings and class discussion

title: Portfolio Theory
description: Introduction to portfolio theories and asset pricing models. Includes mathematical methods of asset pricing, portfolio optimization, and performance measurement and evaluation.

title: Advanced Investment Strategy
description: The course provides a select group of students the opportunity to expand their experience in the process of managing an investment portfolio as a senior analyst.

title: Finance Honors Seminar
description: An in-depth analysis of relevant finance topics adapted to student needs with emphasis on understanding relationships between different areas of finance.

title: Finance Internship
description: Internship experience in business situations relevant to the finance major under supervision of a faculty member.

title: Independent Study Projects
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Beginning Foreign Language I
description: Foundation in a foreign language not regularly offered at UWEC, including listening, speaking, reading, and cultural background.

title: Beginning Foreign Language II
description: Continuation of Foreign Language 101, Beginning Foreign Language I.

title: Foreign Civilization and Culture
description: Introduction to the culture and civilization of a foreign people or society. Students will learn about the society's historical background, its traditions and customs, and its main cultural expressions.

title: Amish in North America
description: Introduction to the history, culture, religion, beliefs, customs, rituals, language, and society of the Old Order Amish in North America, with the goal of enhancing understanding of another and one’s own culture.

title: Intermediate Foreign Language I
description: Review and expansion of grammar and vocabulary, with focus on the development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills, as well as on the culture represented by the language.

title: Intermediate Foreign Language II
description: Continuation of Foreign Language 201 (review and expansion of grammar and vocabulary, with focus on the development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills, as well as on the culture represented by the language).

title: Special Topics in Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages
description: In-depth study of various topics related to the field of Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages (TESOL), which may include Pedagogical Grammar, Psycholinguistics, and ESL Literacy Instruction.

title: Internship Experience
description: This internship course allows students to design international and national field experiences under the supervision of a faculty member. The student may conduct field research or projects, teach a language, or other productive, academic, or professional activity pertinent to their designated focus area.

title: Second Language Acquisition Theory
description: This course will help practicing and prospective teachers to understand and apply current research in second language acquisition. Students will explore the many factors that affect school performance of second language learners.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in a foreign or second language, or introduction to an uncommonly offered foreign language with emphasis on oral proficiency, basic grammar, introduction to reading and writing.

title: Special Topics in Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages
description: In-depth study of various topics related to the field of Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages (TESOL), which may include Pedagogical Grammar, Psycholinguistics, and ESL Literacy Instruction.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in a foreign or second language, or introduction to an uncommonly offered foreign language with emphasis on oral proficiency, basic grammar, introduction to reading and writing.

title: Theories of Second Language Learning and Acquisition
description: The study of linguistic, cognitive, and social factors which influence the development of a second language. Applications to Teaching of English to Speakers of Other Languages (TESOL), bilingual education, and foreign language education.

title: Classical Mythology
description: Greek and Roman myths and legends that have an important place in Western culture. Given in English.

title: Chinese Culture through Films
description: Introduction to Chinese culture through various genres of films and discussion on cultural aspects, with the goal to develop cultural understanding.

title: Latin American Literature in Translation
description: This course introduces students to Latin American authors and their writings in a historical and social context.

title: Readings in Ancient Literature
description: An umbrella course. Intensive reading, discussion, and analysis of literary works from the ancient world in English translation. Specific course offerings might include The Ancient Epic, Greek Tragedy, or Readings in Ancient Greek Literature.

title: Foreign Film as Cultural Expression
description: Foreign film as a distinct medium of artistic expression of the social, historical, cultural values and perspectives of a society.

title: Special Topics
description: In-depth study of a particular topic of interest to faculty and students. Topics may be specific themes, periods, or geographic areas.

title: Chinese Culture through Films
description: Introduction to Chinese culture through various genres of films and discussion on cultural aspects, with the goal to develop cultural understanding.

title: Latin American Literature in Translation
description: This course introduces students to Latin American authors and their writings in a historical and social context.

title: Readings in Ancient Literature
description: An umbrella course. Intensive reading, discussion, and analysis of literary works from the ancient world in English translation. Specific course offerings might include The Ancient Epic, Greek Tragedy, or Readings in Ancient Greek Literature.

title: Beginning French
description: A foundation in listening, speaking, reading, writing, and cultures of the French-speaking world for the acquisition of basic communicative skills.

title: Beginning French
description: A continued foundation in language and culture for the strengthening of basic communicative skills in preparation for the intermediate sequence.

title: Intermediate French
description: A development in listening, speaking, reading, writing, and cultures of the French-speaking world for the acquisition of expanded communicative skills.

title: Intermediate French
description: A continued development in language and culture for the acquisition of expanded communicative skills in preparation for upper division classes.

title: Grammar
description: A refinement of structural language skills through the study of grammatical concepts and vocabulary with contextualized application to reading, writing, and speaking.

title: Pronunciation
description: A study of the spelling-to-sound system through the interpretation of original authentic resources to increase listening and speaking accuracy.

title: Cinema
description: A reinforcement of intermediate-level language skills and cultural competence through film.

title: Professional Communication
description: A focus on specialized language and practices in the French-speaking professional world for increased communicative and cultural competence.

title: Francophone Literature
description: An exposure to literary texts for the development of interpretive skills, cultural competence, and critical reflection.

title: History of France
description: An overview of significant events in the history of France with a focus on political and social developments that have shaped the nation and its people.

title: Modern Society
description: A look into the current institutional systems of France such as government and education that reveal a society’s shared sense of identity.

title: Gastronomy
description: An investigation of the relationship between food and culture in art and literature, with practice to increase communicative skills and cultural competence.

title: Special Topics
description: Study of a theme relevant to the French-speaking world.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in French language, culture, or literature.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Graphic Novels
description: A discovery of remarkable people through graphic novels, a beloved storytelling medium in the French-speaking world.

title: Discussions on Contemporary Societal Issues
description: A development of advanced oral proficiency via conversations and debates on societal issues and roles of French-speaking institutions and individuals.

title: Environmental and Linguistic Diversity
description: A cross-continental exploration of Francophone cultures with a focus on language, history, geography, and challenges to manage and conserve resources.

title: Special Topics
description: In-depth study of a theme relevant to the French-speaking world.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in French language, culture, or literature.

title: Special Topics
description: In-depth study of a theme relevant to the French-speaking world.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects under direction of faculty members.

title: Foundations for Academic Success
description: An integrated program introducing students to University resources and college success strategies. The program assists students in intellectual, personal, and social development to improve retention and academic performance.

title: Strategies for Academic Success
description: Designed to assist student development of successful academic and personal strategies necessary to meet demands at the college level by formulating educational goals, examining effective learning strategies, and making informed decisions.

title: Student Success Seminar
description: This course is an examination of the factors that lead to success in college. Students will learn from their prior college experiences and develop a personalized success plan for achieving their academic goals. Topics include the science of learning, study strategies, goal setting, time management, resilience, and self-regulation.

title: Academic and Career Exploration
description: Designed to give students the skills to make informed choices about their lives, education, and careers. Includes career development theory, interpretations of self-assessment instruments, occupational research techniques, examination of a liberal arts-based education, and goal-setting strategies.

title: Graduate School Exploration
description: This course is designed to help Student Support Services participants make informed decisions pertaining to advanced degrees and to maximize potential for lifelong professional development.

title: Planet Earth: The Physical Environment
description: This course examines the physical processes that shape our environment, which we view as a complex model of interacting systems. Students will learn about the principles and mechanisms of climate and weather, soils, vegetation, ecosystems, and earth surface processes that sculpt physical landscapes. Also examined are the Earth’s systems from a local to global scale as they relate to human interactions with the physical environment.

title: Planet Earth: Human Geography
description: This integrative course examines interactions and relationships between people, places, and environments. It emphasizes how human practices engage with local and global change. The course takes a place-based approach to the analysis of population and health, migration, socioeconomics, identity, language, religion, foodways, economic development, politics, and urbanization.

title: Planet Earth: Our Digital Globe
description: This geography course explores tools and technologies for acquiring, analyzing, managing, and displaying geographic information in the social and environmental sciences. It introduces a variety of geospatial approaches, including geographic information systems (GIS), global positioning systems (GPS), remote sensing (RS), spatial analysis, and cartography (the science and art of map making).

title: Economic Geography
description: This course explores the geographic variations of space, place, location, and scale regarding human economic activities. Issues such as globalization, production, and consumption provide a framework through which the interactions and interdependencies at local and global scales are examined.

title: Planet Earth: Conservation of the Environment
description: This course examines conservation of the environment from a geographical perspective by approaching society’s interdependent relationships with the natural world and its resources. Biophysical, cultural, and socioeconomic issues are addressed in order to better understand environmental problems and develop more effective and sustainable solutions to them.

title: Foundations of Geography
description: An introduction to the foundations, principles and primary geospatial techniques and analyses used in geography.

title: World Regional Geography: Diversity & Globalization
description: Examines the cultural and environmental diversity of our interconnected world using geographical perspectives and tools. Topics include the spatial dimensions of traditional and popular culture, physical environments, nature-society interactions, population distribution and movement, languages and belief systems, socio-economic linkages, politics, urbanization, and other characteristics of the world’s people and places.

title: Introduction to Urban and Regional Planning
description: Basic concepts and philosophies of urban and regional planning, land use, zoning, and sustainable urban development.

title: Introduction to Cartography and Visualization
description: Introduces the art and science of map production through a focus on cartographic design principles, techniques, and best practices. Historic and contemporary maps are examined through the lens of their design elements and cultural context to inform students as they design maps. Discussions centered on the ethics of collecting and analyzing spatial data provide a critical framework for applying cartographic visualization fundamentals in the production of digital reference and thematic map projects.

title: Cultural Landscapes of North America
description: This course introduces ways of seeing and interpreting North American cultures and histories as revealed in our built environments—homes, highways, farms, factories, stores, recreation areas, small towns, city districts, regions, and more. The course encourages students to read landscapes as records of past and present social relations and bearers of cultural meanings. Emphasis is placed on U.S. landscapes.

title: Special Topics
description: Lecture/Discussion Hours: VariableContemporary issues and special topics in geography not typically covered in introductory geography courses. Varies based on interest of students and faculty. May include field trips and experiences.

title: Geography of Western Europe
description: Europe west of the former Soviet Bloc countries, with emphasis on physical, biotic, and cultural themes which unify the region. Includes the national level of regionalization. Utilizes aspects of history, politics, literature, and physical geography.

title: Introduction to Geomorphology
description: An introduction to physical processes that affect the Earth's surface and the landforms that result. The techniques of investigating landforms (field/laboratory) and the fundamentals of communicating the results will be covered.

title: Geography of Russia and Eastern Europe
description: Examination of natural and human resources, economic and political organization of Eastern Europe and the former Soviet Union. Special attention to ideological and institutional problems of population growth, nationalities, and regional development.

title: Space, Society, Subject
description: Broad introduction to social theory in anthropology and geography with an emphasis on contemporary theoretical approaches to social, spatial, and cultural dynamics and their intersections.

title: Culture and Transnational Dynamics
description: Critical discussion of conceptual approaches to cultural processes, differences, and encounters from a transnational perspective.

title: Geography of the Middle East and North Africa
