description: Examination of the physical, cultural, economic, and political geography of the Middle East and North Africa. Emphasis placed on understanding the region's people, places, cultures, nature-society relationships, and the roots of conflict.

title: Geography of Latin America
description: The physical and cultural landscapes of Latin America, including North, Central, and South America and the Caribbean Islands, with attention to the major cultural, economic, social, and political currents of each region.

title: Native Geographies
description: The historical and contemporary relationships of American Indians to place, including land use and environmental issues, Indigenous mapping techniques, sacred sites, territorial nationhood, and legal rights to ceded and sovereign lands. The course also provides insight into the unique cultures of Indigenous peoples around the world.

title: Geography of the United States and Canada
description: This course examines the United States and Canada through a regional analysis of physical landforms, weather and climate, environmental change, and the social, political, economic, and cultural processes that contextualize their dynamic human geographies. Historical and contemporary geographies of culturally diverse groups are emphasized.

title: Geographic Information Systems I
description: This course introduces the basic principles and concepts surrounding the use and application of Geographic Information Systems (GIS). Students will learn how to identify, organize, process, and analyze geospatial data representing geographic features of our world. With the use of state-of-the-art GIS technology, this course makes use of geospatial data representing the environment, economy, socio-cultural, political, and other spheres of our world. Moreover, students will learn how to think spatially, produce maps, analyze geospatial data, and communicate spatial information effectively.

title: Geospatial Field Methods
description: Project-based hands-on learning will teach students field data collection techniques/methods for those interested in a career involving geospatial issues. Traditional and advanced geospatial technology are implemented in the course.

title: Geographic Information Systems II
description: This course expands directly on knowledge gained in Geog 335, Geographic Information Systems I. Focus will be on gaining additional technical skills and applying them to answer more in-depth geospatial questions.

title: Remote Sensing of the Environment
description: Descriptive interpretation of remote-sensing images from ground, aerial, and space platforms.

title: Applied Cartography and Geovisualization
description: This advanced map production course integrates cartographic design principles, techniques, spatial and temporal data analysis, and best practices with creative geovisualization products that analyze environmental and societal issues. Dynamic and interactive geovisualization products, including interactive web maps, infographics, and other visual storytelling projects, are emphasized.

title: Climatology
description: Comprehensive study of climatic phenomena with emphasis on global heat and water balances, daily weather analysis, climate classification, and climate change.

title: Weather and Society
description: This course provides the student with a theoretical and applied understanding of meteorological events and their subsequent impacts upon society.

title: Quaternary Environments
description: Causes, history, and effects of the Ice Ages, including the study of climatic changes, related oceanic and biological disruptions, Ice Age landforms; also, the characteristics and world distribution of modern glaciers.

title: Soils and the Environment
description: Study of soil properties, formation processes, and classification with emphasis on fieldwork, application of soil information to resource management and land use, and technical report writing.

title: Business Geographics
description: Business applications of geographic analysis. Emphasis on population geography, U.S. Census data, location, threshold and market area analysis, retail site location, and location allocation. Use of GIS software, large spatial databases, and database manipulation required.

title: Biogeography
description: This course introduces the identification, description, interpretation, and explanation of historical and contemporary patterns of biogeographical diversity, patterns, and processes on local, regional, and global scales. Geospatial field techniques are employed to collect and analyze local data used to understand the impacts of environmental change.

title: LiDAR Analysis & Applications
description: Introduction to the visualization, processing & use of airborne & terrestrial LiDAR data for applications in environmental & socio-cultural settings.

title: Environmental Hazards
description: The course focuses on environmental hazards and disasters (including geomorphic, atmospheric, and human): how the normal processes of the earth concentrate their energies and deal destructive blows to humans and their structures.

title: Watershed Analysis
description: Watershed-based study of surface-water hydrologic processes and the factors (climate, soils, vegetation, land use) that affect them. Particular emphasis placed on investigations of watersheds in the Eau Claire area.

title: Fluvial Processes and Landforms
description: Study of river processes and the landforms that result from them, including how processes and landforms respond to natural and anthropogenic environmental change. Investigations of streams in the Eau Claire area emphasized.

title: Tourism Geographies
description: This course examines the tourism industry through a spatial and temporal analysis of its various sectors at local, regional, national, and global scales. Topics include social, economic, and environmental sustainability, the development and promotion of natural and cultural destinations in urban and rural locations, tourist supply and demand, niche tourism activities, and modes of transportation used in the tourism industry.

title: Geography International Immersion Experience
description: Place-based analysis using field and interdisciplinary methods to learn about the environments, society and culture of the immersion region. International field sites, experiences and special topics may vary by semester.

title: Landscape Analysis: Cultural
description: Geographic analysis of cultural landscapes by means of field experiences.

title: Geography Field Seminar
description: Place-based analysis using geographic methods and field investigations. Field sites and research topics will vary from semester to semester.

title: Geography of Food
description: Explores the geography and spatial dimensions of food, with attention to the cultures and environments from which they emerged, changing nature/society relationships, and the implications of globalization, economic growth, and technological innovations.

title: Quantitative Methods in Geography
description: An introduction to the quantitative methods commonly used in geography with an emphasis on interpretation of results produced by geospatial and statistical software. Topics include measures of central tendency, mean center, hypothesis testing, regression analysis, spatial analysis, and spatial autocorrelation.

title: Environmental Quality
description: Society's impact on the quality of the environment. The role of cultural attitudes, population, economic systems, technology, and political institutions in creating environmental problems.

title: U.S. Environmental and Sustainability Policy
description: Students will study U.S. environmental and sustainability law and policy to assess the roles of science, key actors, and values in policymaking.

title: International Environmental Problems and Policy
description: Examination of the threats to the global environment and the response of the international community.

title: Geography of Wisconsin
description: A geographic analysis of Wisconsin and its people, with emphasis on cultural and environmental characteristics, patterns of land use, and changing ways of life.

title: Geospatial Applications of UAS
description: This course introduces Unoccupied/Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS) and their applications in the natural and built environment. Topics include UAS sensors and platforms, the ethics of UAS use, sensors calibration and boresighting, flight planning and flying of UAS, data processing software, data quality and accuracy, and the production of ortho-rectified imagery, multispectral imagery, digital terrain, surface models, and other geospatial deliverables.

title: Directed Studies
description: Directed Studies courses are intended for students to investigate topics that are not covered in courses taught within the Department of Geography and Anthropology resulting in a high-quality paper, technical report, creative output, conference presentation, or other assessments deemed appropriate by the instructor.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Independent study courses should focus on a research project or creative activity that addresses a geographic topic and builds upon previous coursework, student-faculty research collaboration, internship, or professional experience. The course will result in a high-quality paper, technical report, creative output, or conference presentation.

title: Intersectional Environmentalism
description: Students will consider multiple ways of understanding both human relationships with the environment and multiple approaches to tackling pressing environmental issues.

title: Geographic Information Systems III
description: This course builds upon the concepts and approaches introduced in Geographic Information Systems II ( GEOG 337 ). Topics may include interpolation, cluster analysis, spatio-temporal data analysis, scripting, and automation. Students will complete a course project in which they identify a spatial problem, collect data, complete an analysis, and produce a professional report.

title: Remote Sensing Data Analytics
description: Explores the principles and techniques of collecting, processing, and extracting information from remotely sensed data to facilitate geospatial intelligence sharing in support of sustainable planning and management.

title: Legal Geographies of Race in the U.S
description: This course provides an introduction to the subdiscipline of legal geography. Explores the interactions between race and the law in the United States, with a specific focus on how U.S. law (re)produces racial inequalities. We examine how U.S. law has constructed race itself and provided differing rights based on racial categories. Students will consider the various institutions and processes that have come together to shape our contemporary racial landscape through the lens of law.

title: Historical Geography
description: Examines how human activities have impacted the natural world, how attitudes toward nature have shifted over time, and how human activities and attitudes have altered our landscape. Emphasis is on United States environmental history.

title: Political Geography
description: A systematic analysis of the distribution of political activity and the relationship of that pattern to other spatial phenomena. Emphasis on national states and international problems.

title: Web Geographic Information Systems
description: An introduction to Web GIS, the programming concepts underpinning construction and implementation of high quality web and mobile solutions.

title: Urban Geography
description: Considers (1) cities as systems of service, trade, and manufacturing centers; (2) the internal structure of cities with emphasis on residential, commercial, and industrial patterns. North American urban places and solutions are stressed.

title: Advanced Special Topics
description: Lecture/Discussion Hours: VariableContemporary issues and special topics in geography. Varies based on interest to students and faculty. May include field trips and experiences.

title: Geography Internship
description: Application of geographic knowledge and skills in supervised professional activities relevant to the student’s area of emphasis. Involves active reflection tied to the student’s academic and career and interests. Option to pursue service learning where applicable.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Independent study courses should focus on a research project or creative activity that addresses a geographic topic and builds upon previous coursework, student-faculty research collaboration, internship, or professional experience. The course will result in a high-quality paper, technical report, creative output, or conference presentation.

title: Space, Society, Subject
description: Broad introduction to social theory in anthropology and geography with an emphasis on contemporary theoretical approaches to social, spatial, and cultural dynamics and their intersections.

title: Culture and Transnational Dynamics
description: Critical discussion of conceptual approaches to cultural processes, differences, and encounters from a transnational perspective.

title: Geography of Latin America
description: The physical and cultural landscapes of Latin America, including North, Central, and South America and the Caribbean Islands, with attention to the major cultural, economic, social, and political currents of each region.

title: Native Geographies
description: The historical and contemporary relationships of American Indians to place, including land use and environmental issues, Indigenous mapping techniques, sacred sites, territorial nationhood, and legal rights to ceded and sovereign lands. The course also provides insight into the unique cultures of Indigenous peoples around the world.

title: Geographic Information Systems I
description: This course introduces the basic principles and concepts surrounding the use and application of Geographic Information Systems (GIS). Students will learn how to identify, organize, process, and analyze geospatial data representing geographic features of our world. With the use of state-of-the-art GIS technology, this course makes use of geospatial data representing the environment, economy, socio-cultural, political, and other spheres of our world. Moreover, students will learn how to think spatially, produce maps, analyze geospatial data, and communicate spatial information effectively.

title: Geospatial Field Methods
description: Project-based hands-on learning will teach students field data collection techniques/methods for those interested in a career involving geospatial issues. Traditional and advanced geospatial technology are implemented in the course.

title: Geographic Information Systems II
description: This course expands directly on knowledge gained in Geog 335, Geographic Information Systems I. Focus will be on gaining additional technical skills and applying them to answer more in-depth geospatial questions.

title: Remote Sensing of the Environment
description: Descriptive interpretation of remote-sensing images from ground, aerial, and space platforms.

title: Soils and the Environment
description: Study of soil properties, formation processes, and classification with emphasis on fieldwork, application of soil information to resource management and land use, and technical report writing.

title: LiDAR Analysis & Applications
description: Introduction to the visualization, processing & use of airborne & terrestrial LiDAR data for applications in environmental & socio-cultural settings.

title: Geography of Wisconsin
description: A geographic analysis of Wisconsin and its people, with emphasis on cultural and environmental characteristics, patterns of land use, and changing ways of life.

title: Geospatial Applications of UAS
description: This course introduces Unoccupied/Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS) and their applications in the natural and built environment. Topics include UAS sensors and platforms, the ethics of UAS use, sensors calibration and boresighting, flight planning and flying of UAS, data processing software, data quality and accuracy, and the production of ortho-rectified imagery, multispectral imagery, digital terrain, surface models, and other geospatial deliverables.

title: Directed Studies
description: Directed Studies courses are intended for students to investigate topics that are not covered in courses taught within the Department of Geography and Anthropology resulting in a high-quality paper, technical report, creative output, conference presentation, or other assessments deemed appropriate by the instructor.

title: Geographic Information Systems III
description: This course builds upon the concepts and approaches introduced in Geographic Information Systems II ( GEOG 337 ). Topics may include interpolation, cluster analysis, spatio-temporal data analysis, scripting, and automation. Students will complete a course project in which they identify a spatial problem, collect data, complete an analysis, and produce a professional report.

title: Web Geographic Information Systems
description: An introduction to Web GIS, the programming concepts underpinning construction and implementation of high quality web and mobile solutions.

title: Independent Study/Graduate
description: An intensive study of a geographic topic resulting in a paper suitable for publication.

title: Oceanography
description: Survey of the world oceans with emphasis on geologic processes. Also includes chemical, physical, and biological aspects of the sea. Investigates human interaction with the marine environment.

title: Earth Science
description: Introduction to basic earth science concepts. Includes study of earth interior and surface processes, earth materials, earth history, earth resources, atmospheric composition and processes, and aspects of the solar system.

title: Physical Geology
description: A study of earth structure, materials, processes, and history; includes discussion of plate tectonics, volcanism, weathering, glaciation, sedimentation, and metamorphism. The course emphasizes the methods of scientific investigation.

title: Environmental Geology
description: Study of the physical environment. Emphasis on earth processes which affect humans such as rivers, erosion, groundwater, landslides, and earthquakes. Methods are examined for reducing or eliminating the harmful effects of human interaction with the geologic environment.

title: Geology of Our National Parks and Monuments
description: A survey of geologic processes and phenomena as exemplified by features of our national parks and monuments. Designed to develop greater appreciation and understanding of natural phenomena.

title: Understanding Earth's Environment
description: This environmental science class will explore the interconnectedness of the geosphere, biosphere, pedosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, and atmosphere, and how humans interact with each of these systems.

title: Introduction to Geology and Environmental Science
description: This course is designed to introduce Geology and Environmental Science majors and minors to the department faculty, to aid in the development of transferrable skills students will need throughout their coursework and their careers. Faculty and upper-level undergraduates will speak on topics including research opportunities, internships, careers. Students will also attend the Earth Science Seminar Series to see presentations by visiting scholars and professional scientists.

title: Special Topics
description: Geologic topics of current, special interest; includes field excursions.

title: Earth Resources and Sustainability
description: Origin, distribution, use, misuse, and conservation of minerals, water, soil, and fuels. Alternative resources and lifestyles for the future are evaluated.

title: Global Environmental Change
description: The composition, structure, and dynamics of the solid earth, oceans, and atmosphere are explored to understand the global geochemical and biochemical cycles that govern the natural response to environmental change on global scales.

title: Water Resources
description: Origin, nature, distribution, movement, exploitation, and conservation of surface and subsurface waters; problems associated with development of water resources in Wisconsin and the U.S.

title: Mineralogy and Petrology I
description: Study of minerals and rocks with an emphasis on common rocks in field settings, hand sample identification, and description of common rocks and minerals. Origin of rocks and minerals and interpretation of processes is stressed.

title: Mineralogy and Petrology II
description: Study of igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks and minerals with an emphasis on laboratory and petrographic analysis.

title: Hydrogeology I
description: The hydrologic cycle, runoff and streamflow, saturated groundwater flow, contaminant transport, geology of groundwater occurrence, aquifer characterization, groundwater flow to wells, hydrogeologic field techniques, groundwater flow and contaminant transport computer modeling, groundwater development and management.

title: Sedimentology and Stratigraphy
description: Origin and evolution of sedimentary rocks, with emphasis on sedimentary processes, stratigraphic principles, and basin evolution.

title: Structural Geology
description: The description and classification of geological structures and the theoretical and physical analysis of rock deformation. Field and laboratory techniques emphasize mapping and the tectonic analysis of geological structures.

title: Introduction to Geochemistry
description: Application of chemistry to geologic problems. Principles of isotope geochemistry, thermodynamics, chemistry of natural waters, and computer modeling of geochemical systems.

title: Analytical Geochemistry Laboratory
description: Theory and application of geochemical instrumentation including x-ray diffraction, x-ray microanalysis, scanning electron microscopy, atomic absorption spectrometry, mass spectrometry, and neutron activation analysis.

title: Geological Field Excursion
description: Extended travel to study field geology. Students record notes and data in the field and submit final written and/or oral reports. Special course fee to cover costs. Travel proposal must receive approval of department faculty.

title: Geomorphology and Aerial Photography Interpretation
description: A process-oriented analysis of landforms and applied geomorphology. Interpretation of landforms using topographic maps and aerial photographs will be emphasized. Communicating scientific material to expert audiences also will be stressed.

title: Economic Mineral Deposits
description: Origin, association, and exploitation of metallic and nonmetallic mineral deposits. Study of ore suites from selected mining districts and methods of evaluating ore deposits.

title: Geologic Immersion Experience
description: Project-based, field intensive geologic immersion experience focusing on the physical, biologic, geomorphic, and tectonic evolution of a geologic province and on societal interaction with the geologic setting.

title: Directed Studies
description: Individual research or study for the advanced geology major who seeks to develop specialized knowledge and skills. Course proposals are student-initiated.

title: Student Academic Apprenticeship
description: Faculty members will select and supervise qualified students to facilitate teaching and learning in a Geology and Environmental Science course. Students enhance their knowledge of course material and their ability to communicate that knowledge.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual research or studies of distinctive nature.

title: Hydrogeology II
description: Review of principles of groundwater flow and contaminant transport, reactive groundwater contamination, groundwater remediation, chemical evolution of natural groundwater, flow in the unsaturated zone, advanced groundwater flow and contaminant transport computer modeling, water law.

title: Global Tectonics
description: Applying observations of geologic field relations, basin analysis, paleontology, and orogenesis to study the origin and evolution of Earth within the context of the theory of plate tectonics.

title: Glacial Geology
description: A process-oriented analysis of glacial landforms and sediments. Topographic maps, aerial photographs, and field mapping projects will be used to interpret glacial landforms, sediments, and to solve glacial geologic problems.

title: MSHA New Miner Training
description: New Miner Safety Training (MSHA Part 46, 24 hours) discusses mine safety, hazard awareness and mine protocol, and is federally required to work at surficial mine sites.

title: Responsible Mining Seminar
description: Industry speakers will discuss environmental and political aspects of responsible mining. Preparation for internships and jobs will be stressed.

title: Applied Geophysics
description: Acquisition, processing, and interpretation of geophysical data including seismic, gravity, magnetic, GPR, resistivity, and electromagnetic methods.

title: Geological Field Methods
description: Introduction to fundamental geological field methods and digitization of field data. Topics include basic mapping skills, identifying fossils, collecting and recording structural and stratigraphic data, and digitizing field data using vector graphics software and Geographic Information System mapping technology.

title: Field Geology I
description: Introduction to geologic mapping techniques, with an emphasis on construction of geologic maps and cross-sections in mildly deformed sedimentary and volcanic rocks.

title: Field Geology II
description: Introduction to advanced geologic field techniques, with an emphasis on construction of geologic maps and cross-sections in deformed plutonic, volcanic, metamorphic, and sedimentary rocks.

title: Field Hydrology
description: This three-week course involves hydrologic field studies in western Wisconsin, such as groundwater and aquifer characterization, wetland delineation, evaluation of aquatic ecosystems, and water quality assessment.

title: Advanced Special Topics
description: Topics of special interest and current relevance to the advanced geology student; includes field excursions. Topics vary.

title: Geology Internship
description: Provides supervised on-the-job experience in a geologically-related area of interest.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Hydrogeology I
description: The hydrologic cycle, runoff and streamflow, saturated groundwater flow, contaminant transport, geology of groundwater occurrence, aquifer characterization, groundwater flow to wells, hydrogeologic field techniques, groundwater flow and contaminant transport computer modeling, groundwater development and management.

title: Introduction to Geochemistry
description: Application of chemistry to geologic problems. Principles of isotope geochemistry, thermodynamics, chemistry of natural waters, and computer modeling of geochemical systems.

title: Analytical Geochemistry Laboratory
description: Theory and application of geochemical instrumentation including x-ray diffraction, x-ray microanalysis, scanning electron microscopy, atomic absorption spectrometry, mass spectrometry, and neutron activation analysis.

title: Hydrogeology II
description: Review of principles of groundwater flow and contaminant transport, reactive groundwater contamination, groundwater remediation, chemical evolution of natural groundwater, flow in the unsaturated zone, advanced groundwater flow and contaminant transport computer modeling, water law.

title: Applied Geophysics
description: Acquisition, processing, and interpretation of geophysical data including seismic, gravity, magnetic, GPR, resistivity, and electromagnetic methods.

title: Advanced Special Topics
description: Topics of special interest and current relevance to the advanced geology student; includes field excursions. Topics vary.

title: Directed Studies
description: Permits groups of students to study topical areas in an intensive way under the direction of department faculty members.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects under direction of faculty members.

title: Beginning German
description: A foundation in listening, speaking, reading, writing, and culture.

title: Beginning German
description: Continuation of German 101. A foundation in listening, speaking, reading, writing, and culture.

title: Intermediate German I
description: Review of standard German grammar and vocabulary with focus on the development of speaking, writing, reading, and listening skills. Students gain an understanding of German culture, literature, and current events through a variety of topics.

title: Intermediate German II
description: Continuation of German 201.

title: Grammar Review and Composition
description: Continued development and refinement of structural language skills through review of German morphology, syntax, vocabulary, and style.

title: Oral Proficiency
description: This course focuses on the advanced-level language functions of the ACTFL Proficiency Guidelines (American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages), including conversing, narrating, describing in paragraph-length discourse.

title: German for Professional Communication
description: This course focuses on the development of practical language skills and functional competence in a German-speaking professional environment. Course language is German.

title: The German Sound System
description: Theory of the German sound system and intensive practice of German pronunciation and intonation. Course language is German.

title: Introduction to Translation
description: A practically oriented introduction into the craft of translation, with intensive practice of text translation, mainly from German to English. Discussion of translation techniques and some readings of translation theory.

title: Modern Germany: Literature, History, Culture
description: Combines German history survey of the modern era, including work on contemporary images and documents, with close reading of literary/cultural texts and close attention to students' professional development in reading, writing, listening, speaking, translating. Course language is German.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in German language, culture, or literature.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Structure of German in Contrast
description: The course aims to refine students' proficiency in the German language as well as to further students' "conscious" mastery of German through a comparative/contrastive analysis with English.

title: Special Topics
description: In-depth study of a special area of language, culture, history, or literature. Topics and credits will vary depending on interests of students and faculty.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: The German Sound System
description: Theory of the German sound system and intensive practice of German pronunciation and intonation. Course language is German.

title: Introduction to Translation
description: A practically oriented introduction into the craft of translation, with intensive practice of text translation, mainly from German to English. Discussion of translation techniques and some readings of translation theory.

title: Modern Germany: Literature, History, Culture
description: Combines German history survey of the modern era including work on contemporary images and documents, with close reading of literary/cultural texts and close attention to students' professional development in reading, writing, listening, speaking, translating. Course language is German.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of a problem or special area in German language, culture, or literature.

title: Structure of German in Contrast
description: The course aims to refine students' proficiency in the German language as well as to further students' "conscious" mastery of German through a comparative/contrastive analysis with English.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects under direction of faculty members.

title: Beginning Classical Greek
description: Introduction to the Classical Greek language, with emphasis on grammar, analytic, and translation skills. The course will serve as an introduction to ancient Greek culture and its impacts on modern, western European and American civilizations.

title: Beginning Classical Greek
description: Continuation of Greek 101.

title: Intermediate Classical Greek I
description: Review of Classical Greek grammar and vocabulary with focus on translation skills. Students will gain a developed understanding of ancient Greek culture and its impact.

title: Introduction to Health Care Administration
description: Survey of the field of health and aging services and the functions of various administrative roles. Provides experiences upon which to base decisions about health services administration as a career.

title: Overview of the U.S. Health Care System
description: A multidisciplinary course addressing issues that affect policy and delivery systems in health care. The course seeks to orient the student to the spectrum of issues and the dynamics of the U.S. health care system.

title: Multidisciplinary Perspectives on Aging
description: This course presents a multidisciplinary overview of critical issues related to aging. Research and theory supplement an exploration of issues and career opportunities in gerontology, from both research and service provider orientations.

title: Special Topics
description: A variable content course consisting of topics of special interest to students and faculty that are not covered in other health care administration courses.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Foundations of Quality Management in Health Service
description: An exploration of the technical, managerial and organizational issues that are important in understanding and implementing quality management systems in health service organizations.

title: Leadership and Management Practices in Health Services
description: An exploration of necessary administrative skills of health services leadership including: strategic planning; quality management; financial management; human resources management; government relations; marketing; and the ability to apply these concepts within a health system.

title: Health Care Reimbursement and Financial Management
description: Students will explore healthcare reimbursement systems, revenue and expense cycle management, budgeting, financial analysis and reporting, and other key financial decision-making strategies. Emphasis is placed on financial management tools and approaches to strengthen healthcare organizations’ operational performance and ensure the delivery of high-quality care and services within a sustainable business model.

title: Administrative Residency I
description: The student undertakes prescribed activities and rotations with defined experiences and learning goals to develop a basic understanding of operational, regulatory, and environmental stands of resident service departments and functions.

title: Administrative Residency II
description: Building on previous skills, the student continues participation in prescribed activities and rotations with defined experiences and learning goals to further understanding of operational, regulatory, and environmental standards of resident service departments and functions.

title: Administrative Residency III
description: Building on previous skills, the student continues participation in prescribed activities and rotations with defined experiences and learning goals to achieve competency in required operational, regulatory, and environmental standards of resident service departments and functions.

title: Resident Service and Supports in Health Care
description: Examines the process of care delivery and roles of interdisciplinary care teams, while emphasizing the health care leader's role and responsibility in prioritizing and operationalizing person-centered care to measurably improve quality of care and quality of life for care recipients.

title: Quality Management Applications in Health Care
description: Students gain an awareness of quality management principles and practices in health care settings and learn how to use various quality tools for effective problem identification and solving.

title: Human Resources Management in Health Services
description: Students learn the basic goals and principles of human resources, understand the driving forces of human resource outcomes, and gain an awareness for the application of human resources practices in health care today.

title: Data Driven Health Care Decision Making
description: Students develop an understanding of how to identify, prioritize, and use data and information to help them strategically manage a health and aging services organization.

title: Financial Management in Health Care
description: Applies core principles of financial management to real-world health care settings, with emphasis on budgeting, reimbursement, and cost control. Students will examine, compare, and compile strategies for effective financial decision-making while sustaining quality care within the fiscally constraints of a health care environment.

title: Marketing and Public Relations in Health Services
description: Students are exposed to the basic goals and principles of an effective marketing and public relations program in health care and participate in the application of a variety of marketing strategies.

title: Health Care Rules and Regulations
description: Students develop an understanding of the current laws, rules and regulations that impact long-term care organizations. Students will learn strategies for proactively managing an organization within the context of a regulatory environment.

title: Health Care Management Competencies
description: This course will examine in-depth management competencies necessary to successfully operate a health care organization. Students will also gain an understanding of the necessary professional functions and skills to successfully serve in an administrative role in a health care organization.

title: Leadership and Prof Development in Health Services
description: The initial focus is on the practice of personal self-examination, including the development of strengths and the awareness of limitations. Students will explore their own level of ability in each of these critical areas of management and begin to focus on their own enhanced management proficiency.

title: Organizational Leadership Practices in Health Care
description: Emphasis is on the development and execution of the key strategic leadership practices required of organizational leaders. Students will practice their leadership expertise with selected projects. Additionally, they will learn from their shared experiences with their administrative residency cohort.

title: Special Topics
description: A variable content course consisting of advanced topics of special interest to students and faculty that are not covered in other health care administration courses.

title: Directed Studies
description: Permits groups of students to pursue a defined, organized study of a topic under the direction of a department of public health professions staff member.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study projects under the direction of a faculty member.

title: United States History to 1877
description: Survey from colonial settlement through the Civil War and Reconstruction. Provides a basis for better understanding of American political, institutional, economic, social, and intellectual development.

title: United States History Since 1877
description: Survey with emphasis on concepts and topics considered essential to an understanding of institutions and issues of public concern today.

title: World History to 1500
description: How global processes such as the spread of agriculture, the growth of long-distance commerce, the rise of cities, the establishment of empires, and the development of religions shaped the lives of men and women from 10,000 B.C.E. to 1500 C.E.

title: World History Since 1500
description: A global history of humankind. Emphasis on the growth of international commerce, the rise and fall of empires, industrialization and deindustrialization, and changes in types of governance, belief systems, gender structures, and the environment.

title: Modern East and Southeast Asia
description: A survey of modern Asian history from around 1600 to the present focusing on Chinese, Japanese, and Hmong people, with attention to cultural, social, and political history.

title: Latin American History: Colonial to the Present
description: Survey covering the major political, social, and economic developments and issues in Latin America’s historical formation, from the colonial era to the present.

title: Doing History: Skills and Practice
description: Students will learn and hone skills in historical thinking, primary and secondary source analysis, communication, and career and professional development. Students also build community with other first-year students, upper-class mentors, and department faculty. Course is intended for incoming History Majors and Social Studies Teaching, History Emphasis Majors.

title: American Women's History
description: A survey of the history of women from precolumbian America to the present. Provides a basis for understanding the historical experience of women from diverse cultural origins including Native American, African American, Asian American, Latin American, and Euro American women.

title: African American History 1865 to Present
description: Course includes information on lynching, the Civil Rights Movement, and race relations.

title: Hmong and America
description: This course will trace the history of Hmong peoples from China through the war in Laos to America and the newer diasporas.

title: American Indian History
description: An introduction to the history of the native peoples of the United States with major emphasis upon European contact, the mutual interaction of European and American Indian cultures, and the resulting adaptations and conflicts.

title: History Travel Seminar
description: An opportunity for students to visit the locations of an historical event or period. Extensive reading and orientation sessions required prior to departure. Specific locales will change depending on student and faculty interest.

title: The Nazis and Germany, 1914-1945
description: Background, emergence, and totalitarian practices of National Socialism in Germany.

title: History of World War II
description: Overview of the World War II era, ca. 1933-1945, examining root causes, conduct, and outcomes of the war from a world perspective.

title: Introduction to Research Methods and the Profession
description: Introduces students to the study of history as a scholarly discipline and as a foundation for careers. Identifies theories, concepts, methodologies, and historiographic traditions used by historians. Assignments will enhance research, writing, oral communications, bibliographic and statistical skills.

title: Origins of Current World Conflicts
description: Topical course exploring historical origins of current world conflicts. Causes and forms of conflict include evolution of human rights, ethnic-racial and class divisions, imperialism and decolonization, nationalism, political ideologies, total war, terrorism and comparative revolutions. Film Night Required.

title: Ancient Near Eastern Civilizations
description: In-depth survey of the political, social, and cultural history of ancient Mesopotamia, Egypt, Syro-Palestine, Anatolia, and Iran from the rise of cities to Alexander the Great.

title: Greek Civilization
description: A history of the Greeks beginning with the Bronze Age and continuing through the Alexandrian or Hellenistic period. Stress on cultural contributions of each successive period.

title: Roman Civilization
description: A history of the Romans beginning with the Etruscan civilization after 1000 B.C. and continuing through the Empire Age to the end of the third century.

title: Ancient Historiography
description: In-depth examination of the works of selected ancient historians, their methodology, purpose, and place in the tradition.

title: Gender, Sex, and Science in European History
description: This course examines recent scholarship in European history on gender, sexuality, science, and the body. It focuses on the historical construction of gender and sexuality, the gendering of science, inclusion/exclusion in academic and scientific work.

title: Studies in Asian/Pacific World History
description: Selected topics in Asian history or the history of transnational processes, persons, and events within and across the Pacific Ocean. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in the History of Race in the U.S
description: Selected topics in the history of U.S. racial/ethnic minorities. Topics can include aspects of Asian American, African American, and/or Latino American history. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in Global and Comparative History
description: Selected topics in global and comparative history. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in the History of the Americas
description: Selected topics in the history of the Americas. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region/nation, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: History and Memory in Europe
description: Introduction to the study of collective memory, with particular emphasis on Europe. Themes vary based on instructor, but may include collective memories and nationalism, imperialism, World War I & World War II, the Holocaust, Cold War, and Post-Communist experience.

title: Twentieth Century Europe, 1900 to Present
description: Political, economic, intellectual, and social developments in major European states. Special attention to the two World Wars, Russian Revolution, fascism, Cold War, Europe's loss of overseas power, World War II, and postwar integration.

title: The Era of the American Revolution
description: Conflicting forces which produced breakdown of Anglo-American political system, war years, social and political readjustment, and framing of new governments.

title: Sectionalism, Civil War, and Reconstruction
description: America's most bitter and bloody war. Focus on three topics: why the war broke out when it did; how the Union won the war and destroyed a slave society; and why, after winning the war, the North lost the peace of Reconstruction.

title: Recent US History
description: America in 1945, "Cold War," Eisenhower years, violent 1960s, Vietnam War, Watergate, Carter Presidency, Reagan Presidency.

title: African American Civil Rights Movement, 1914-Present
description: African American civil rights movement activity focusing on organized and unorganized responses to discrimination in the twentieth century.

title: Civil Rights and American Memory
description: This course explores the public memory of the civil rights movement through class lecture, discussion and participation in UW-Eau Claire's Civil Rights Pilgrimage. Students will examine the importance of individual agency, historical contingency, and social change.

title: American Environmental History
description: Examines interactions between human societies and the natural world in what is now the United States. Through readings and several field trips students will learn the essential elements of American Environmental History.

title: History of Public Health in the United States
description: Introduces students to the key features of the history of public health in the United States, focusing primarily on developments in the twentieth and twenty first centuries. Organized around case studies of significant achievements in public health.

title: The Global History of Disease
description: This class explores the global history of disease from ancient times to the present. Includes social, political, and cultural aspects of the history of disease.

title: The American West
description: Frontier movement, problems, procedures, and attitudes of frontier areas, and influence of the West.

title: Modern France
description: Social, political, and cultural development of France from the French Revolution of 1789 to the present. Topics include industrialization, civil society, democratization, art and society, World War I and II, French imperialism, women and gender.

title: Modern Germany
description: Since 1848, with emphasis on Bismarckian, Wilhelmian, Weimar, Nazi, and postwar period.

title: Historical Roots of Health Disparities in the Americas
description: This course explores the historical roots of health disparities by examining how the intersection of race, gender, sex, class, and medicine in the Atlantic world and Americas led to the creation of structural inequalities in medicine and society that impact people today.

title: Studies in American Indian History
description: Selected topics depending on student and staff interests. Possible topics include the history of Wisconsin Indians and American Indians in the twentieth century.

title: Wisconsin American Indian History
description: Study of American Indian communities in Wisconsin with emphasis on the mix of continuity and change in response to outside forces.

title: History of the Family in America
description: Evolution of the American family from colonial times to the present. Emphasis on child raising, gender roles, the economic function of the family, the emergence of adolescence, the importance of kinship, and marriage practices.

title: World War I and II Military
description: Explores World Wars I and II from the perspective of professional military history. Emphasizes strategy, tactics, decision-making, and battle history. The role of culture's influence on strategy is discussed.

title: American War in the Post 1945 World
description: American involvement in the Korean and Vietnam conflicts, military policy in between wars, Desert Shield/Desert Storm, and the home front impact of war. Emphasizes the daily lives of combatants.

title: Museums in the United States
description: Examines artifact analysis, museum history and methods. Students will design and install an exhibit at a local museum during the semester.

title: Comparative Genocide
description: Examines the causes and implementation of genocide as well as strategies for preventing and combating genocidal hatred, especially through international law and institutions. Cases examined will be drawn from multiple time periods and global locations.

title: Imperialism Since 1800
description: Examines the complex history of empire-building overseas, concentrating upon the era of "high" imperialism from the mid-nineteenth century through to the present. Particular focus on issues of race, gender and culture.

title: Modern Africa
description: Examines the major components of Modern African history. Among topics for study are African civilizations, European contact, slavery, colonialism, independence movements, African nationalism as well as more recent political, social, cultural and economic change.

title: The Vietnam War
description: Examination of the conflicts in Vietnam, 1945-1975. Topics include: Vietnam in history, French colonial rule, nationalism and communism in Vietnam, U.S. policies, the debates of the war, and the legacy of the war.

title: Introduction to Public History
description: This course will introduce students to the theory and practice of a variety of history-related pursuits known collectively as public history because they are primarily aimed at audiences outside the university. Students will learn about a range of career options available to public historians, as well as the special challenges that they confront.

title: History of China
description: Social, cultural, and political history of China, inclusive of women, ethnic minorities, and commoners, from the ancient period to the present.

title: History of Japan
description: Social, economic, political, and intellectual development of Japan: emergence of Japanese civilization, the Heian period, the age of the samurai, the Meiji Restoration, and Japan in the twentieth century.

title: History of Antisemitism
description: Global history of antisemitism from ancient world to the present, religious roots; economic and racial origins; impact of Zionism, Holocaust and Arab-Israeli conflict.

title: Roots of Selected Contemporary World Problems
description: Selected world problems depending on student interest and staff. Number of credits assigned will vary according to the nature of the topic. Consult Class Schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in the History of Women
description: Selected aspects depending on student interest and staff. Number of credits assigned will vary according to nature of topic. Possible topics include a survey of entire history of women in Western world or women in American history. Consult Class Schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Directed Studies
description: Study of historical problem or topic.

title: Research Apprenticeship in History
description: Qualified students, recruited and supervised by an instructor, engage in original research in collaboration with the instructor. Research methods and current status of historical knowledge are applied to create new knowledge.

title: Student Academic Apprenticeship in History
description: Qualified students work under the close supervision of faculty to tutor students. Students typically complete a reflection or related project as a part of the experience.

title: Independent Study - Juniors
description: Intensive study of topic resulting in a quality research paper or report.

title: Public History in Great Britain
description: In this course, students are introduced to historic preservation and interpretation as they are carried out in Great Britain. The course is conducted in Great Britain through a summer international immersion experience.

title: Seminar in Public History
description: The seminar in public history is organized around completing a project in a particular field in the discipline. Students will conduct research and complete a written seminar paper that will serve as a part of a larger class product.

title: Research Seminar
description: Capstone experience in which the students complete a major research project for presentation to the class and evaluation by the history faculty. The project will reflect the writing, reading, and analytical skills that the students have developed in the history major.

title: Special Topics
description: Topics will vary depending on interest of students and faculty. Topics may be specific themes or eras of history.

title: Honors Seminar in History
description: Intensive study of selected problems. Exact topics covered will vary according to particular interests of professor, degree of preparedness of students, and availability of materials.

title: Teaching by Doing History
description: Students learn how to incorporate inclusive historical content and primary sources into secondary school curricula and classroom active learning strategies, such as digital humanities.

title: Honors Independent Study
description: Involves independent reading with regular conferences between student and faculty member, or a research paper with similar regular conferences.

title: Internship
description: Supervised assignment to museum, archives, historical site, public agency, or private business for practical experience in the work of public historians. Work will build on and be related to research skills and historical knowledge developed in other history courses.

title: Independent Study - Seniors
description: Intensive study of topic resulting in a quality research paper or report.

title: Ancient Near Eastern Civilizations
description: In-depth survey of the political, social, and cultural history of ancient Mesopotamia, Egypt, Syro-Palestine, Anatolia, and Iran from the rise of cities to Alexander the Great.

title: Greek Civilization
description: A history of the Greeks beginning with the Bronze Age and continuing through the Alexandrian or Hellenistic period. Stress on cultural contributions of each successive period.

title: Roman Civilization
description: A history of the Romans beginning with the Etruscan civilization after 1000 B.C. and continuing through the Empire Age to the end of the third century.

title: Ancient Historiography
description: In-depth examination of the works of selected ancient historians, their methodology, purpose, and place in the tradition.

title: Gender, Sex, and Science in European History
description: This course examines recent scholarship in European history on gender, sexuality, science, and the body. It focuses on the historical construction of gender and sexuality, the gendering of science, inclusion/exclusion in academic and scientific work.

title: Studies in Asian/Pacific World History
description: Selected topics in Asian history or the history of transnational processes, persons, and events within and across the Pacific Ocean. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in the History of Race in the U.S
description: Selected topics in the history of U.S. racial/ethnic minorities. Topics can include aspects of Asian America, African American, and/or Latino American history. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in Global and Comparative History
description: Selected topics in global and comparative history. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in the History of the Americas
description: Selected topics in the history of the Americas. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region/nation, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: Studies in the History of the Americas
description: Selected topics in the history of the Americas. Topics may focus on a particular time period, region/nation, or historical process. Consult class schedule or department office for current offering.

title: History and Memory in Europe
description: Introduction to the study of collective memory, with particular emphasis on Europe. Themes vary based on instructor, but may include collective memories and nationalism, imperialism, World War I & World War II, the Holocaust, Cold War, and Post-Communist experience.

title: Twentieth Century Europe, 1900 to Present
description: Political, economic, intellectual, and social developments in major European states. Special attention to the two World Wars, Russian Revolution, fascism, Cold War, Europe's loss of overseas power, World War II, and postwar integration.

title: The Era of the American Revolution
description: Conflicting forces which produced breakdown of Anglo-American political system, war years, social and political readjustment, and framing of new governments.

title: Sectionalism, Civil War, and Reconstruction
description: America's most bitter and bloody war. Focus on three topics: why the war broke out when it did; how the Union won the war and destroyed a slave society; and why, after winning the war, the North lost the peace of Reconstruction.

title: Recent US History
description: America in 1945, "Cold War," Eisenhower years, violent 1960s, Vietnam War, Watergate, Carter Presidency, Reagan Presidency.

title: African American Civil Rights Movement, 1914-Present
description: African American civil rights movement activity focusing on organized and unorganized responses to discrimination in the twentieth century.

title: Civil Rights and American Memory
description: This course explores the public memory of the civil rights movement through class lecture, discussion and participation in UW-Eau Claire's Civil Rights Pilgrimage. Students will examine the importance of individual agency, historical contingency, and social change.

title: American Environmental History
description: Examines interactions between human societies and the natural world in what is now the United States. Through readings and several field trips students will learn the essential elements of American Environmental History.

title: History of Public Health in the United States
description: Introduces students to the key features of the history of public health in the United States, focusing primarily on developments in the twentieth and twenty first centuries. Organized around case studies of significant achievements in public health.

title: The Global History of Disease
description: This class explores the global history of disease from ancient times to the present. Includes social, political, and cultural aspects of the history of disease.

title: The American West
description: Frontier movement, problems, procedures, and attitudes of frontier areas, and influence of the West.

title: Modern France
description: Social, political, and cultural development of France from the French Revolution of 1789 to the present. Topics include industrialization, civil society, democratization, art and society, World War I and II, French imperialism, women and gender.

title: Modern Germany
description: Since 1848, with emphasis on Bismarckian, Wilhelmian, Weimar, Nazi, and postwar period.

title: Historical Roots of Health Disparities in the Americas
description: This course explores the historical roots of health disparities by examining how the intersection of race, gender, sex, class, and medicine in the Atlantic world and Americas led to the creation of structural inequalities in medicine and society that impact people today.

title: Studies in American Indian History
description: Selected topics depending on student and staff interests. Possible topics include the history of Wisconsin Indians and American Indians in the twentieth century.

title: Wisconsin American Indian History
description: Study of American Indian communities in Wisconsin with emphasis on the mix of continuity and change in response to outside forces.

title: History of the Family in America
description: Evolution of the American family from colonial times to the present. Emphasis on child raising, gender roles, the economic function of the family, the emergence of adolescence, the importance of kinship, and marriage practices.

title: History of the Family in America
description: Evolution of the American family from colonial times to the present. Emphasis on child raising, gender roles, the economic function of the family, the emergence of adolescence, the importance of kinship, and marriage practices.

title: World War I and II Military
description: Explores World Wars I and II from the perspective of professional military history. Emphasizes strategy, tactics, decision-making, and battle history. The role of culture's influence on strategy is discussed.

title: World War I and II Military
description: Explores World Wars I and II from the perspective of professional military history. Emphasizes strategy, tactics, decision-making, and battle history. The role of culture's influence on strategy is discussed.

title: American War in the Post 1945 World
description: American involvement in the Korean and Vietnam conflicts, military policy in between wars, Desert Shield/Desert Storm, and the home front impact of war. Emphasizes the daily lives of combatants.

title: Museums in the United States
description: Examines artifact analysis, museum history and methods. Students will design and install an exhibit at a local museum during the semester.

title: Museums in the United States
description: Examines artifact analysis, museum history and methods. Students will design and install an exhibit at a local museum during the semester.

title: Comparative Genocide
description: Examines the causes and implementation of genocide as well as strategies for preventing and combating genocidal hatred, especially through international law and institutions. Cases examined will be drawn from multiple time periods and global locations.

description: Familiarizes students with major issues of historical debate in the field by reading a book per week or commensurate articles. Term paper summarizing and evaluating views on a specific issue is shared with other students.

title: Studies in History for Teachers
description: A concentrated study of an historical topic for one or more weeks, depending on number of credits for which course is offered.

title: Reading Historical Texts
description: Readings of historical texts in-translation or original language.

title: Readings in U.S. History
description: Familiarizes students with major issues of historical debate in the field by reading a book per week or commensurate articles. Term paper summarizing and evaluating views on a specific issue is shared with other students.

title: Colloquium in History
description: Various topics are offered as indicated in the current Class Schedule. Emphasizes mastery of major secondary literature. A paper treating the literature on a specific subject is required.

title: Readings in Area of Thesis
description: Independent study format. Reading in depth in area of concentration for thesis or research paper. Thesis/research paper adviser is instructor. Culminates in presentation of prospectus to thesis committee.

title: Readings in Area of Thesis
description: Independent study format with discussion. Reading in depth in area of concentration for thesis or research paper. Thesis/research paper adviser is instructor. Culminates in presentation of prospectus to thesis committee.

title: Seminar in History
description: Various topics are offered as indicated in the schedule of classes. Introduces students to secondary literature on a topic as preparation for research in original sources leading to the writing of a paper to be presented to the class.

title: Internship in Public History
description: Supervised assignment to museum, archives, historical site, or other agency for working experience as a public historian. Work will apply research skills and knowledge of history to help solve community problems.

title: Current Trends in History
description: Examines the emergence of history as a profession, major subfields today within the discipline of history, methodologies and models currently influencing the work of historians, and dominant issues presently engaging the attention of historians.

title: Current Trends in History
description: Examines the emergence of history as a profession, major subfields today within the discipline of history, methodologies and models currently influencing the work of historians, and dominant issues presently engaging the attention of historians.

title: Public History Thesis Practicum
description: Practicum course designed to allow students to develop a public audience for their original research and to develop working experience as a public historian. In combination with HIST 799 , this course constitutes a public history thesis option.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual project under the direction of a faculty member.

title: Thesis
description: A description of acceptable topics and the precise nature of the thesis requirement is provided in the departmental program descriptions.

title: Beginning Hmong I
description: An introduction to Hmong language and culture. Students will gain a basic understanding of the Hmong language structure in writing, reading, grammar, and vocabulary and be exposed to Hmong traditions and customs.

title: Beginning Hmong II
description: Continuation of Hmong Language I. Study of grammar and culture with emphasis on reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills. Students gain greater understanding and appreciation of Hmong culture, literature, and current events through a variety of topics.

title: Hmong American Experiences in the U.S
description: An interdisciplinary examination of Hmong American refugee migration, history, politics, cultural transformations, and contemporary issues in the post-1975 era. Additional topics addressed may include race, religion, family, identity, gender, sexuality, education, and/or media.

title: Critical Hmong Studies Domestic Travel Seminar
description: In-depth classroom experience accompanied by domestic travel to locations relevant to the study of Hmong history, language, politics, and culture. Extensive reading and interdisciplinary research projects may be assigned or conducted during the pre-departure and/or domestic intercultural immersion experience phases.

title: Critical Hmong Studies International Travel Seminar
description: In-depth classroom experience accompanied by international travel to locations relevant to the study of Hmong history, language, politics, and culture. Extensive reading and interdisciplinary research projects may be assigned or conducted during the pre-departure and/or international immersion experience phases.

title: Intersectionality: Critical Approaches to Identity and Injustice
description: Uses an interdisciplinary humanities approach to critically examine the concept of intersectionality and its applications and implications on identities, historical and contemporary injustice, and social issues.

title: Special Topics in Critical Hmong Studies
description: In-depth study of a special topic related to the history, literature, language, culture, politics, and/or experiences of Hmong Americans or Hmong living in the larger global diaspora.

title: First-Year Honors Seminar
description: Introduction to the University and to expectations of an Honors baccalaureate degree. Explores the value of a liberal education, the skills and knowledge needed to be an educated person, the roles of the Honors College and university requirements.
